blocks:
Ayahs

الٓر ۚ كِتَـٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ إِلَيْكَ لِتُخْرِجَ ٱلنَّاسَ مِنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَمِيدِ (١)
Alif, Lam, Ra. [This is] a Book We have sent down to you that you may bring mankind out from darkness into light, by the command of their Lord, to the path of the All-mighty, the All-laudable (1)
ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَوَيْلٌ لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ مِنْ عَذَابٍ شَدِيدٍ (٢)
—Allah, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Woe to the faithless for a severe punishment (2)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَحِبُّونَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا عَلَى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَيَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (٣)
—those who prefer the life of this world to the Hereafter, and bar [others] from the way of Allah, and seek to make it crooked. They are in extreme error. (3)

Content of the Surah

As is evident from the name of the Surah, a portion of it was revealed concerning the hero of monotheism, Ibrahim (Abraham), the breaker of idols (specifically the section regarding his supplications).

Another part of this Surah refers to the history of previous prophets such as Nuh (Noah), Musa (Moses), and the peoples of ‘Ad and Thamud, along with the cautionary lessons embedded within them.

The combination of these elements completes the extensive discussions revealed in this Surah regarding exhortation, advice, glad tidings, and warnings.

And as we read in most "Meccan Surahs," a significant portion also discusses the "Origin" (Mabda') and the "Resurrection" (Ma'ad). When faith in these becomes firmly rooted in a person's heart, their soul, spirit, and subsequently their speech and actions, acquire a different light and radiance, aligning them with the path of Truth and Allah.

In summary, this Surah is a collection of the expression of beliefs, counsels, exhortations, and the cautionary life stories of past nations, as well as an explanation of the purpose of the prophets' missions and the revelation of Heavenly Books.

The Virtue of this Surah

It is narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said:

مَنْ قَرَأَ سُورَةَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَ الْحِجْرِ أُعْطِيَ مِنَ الْأَجْرِ عَشْرَ حَسَنَاتٍ بِعَدَدِ مَنْ عَبَدَ الْأَصْنَامَ وَ بِعَدَدِ مَنْ لَمْ يَعْبُدْهَا:

"Whoever recites Surah Ibrahim and al-Hijr, Allah will grant him ten good deeds for every person who worshipped idols and every person who did not worship them." [^1]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan and Nur al-Thaqalayn at the beginning of this Surah.

As we have stated many times, the rewards mentioned regarding the recitation of the Surahs of the Quran are rewards for recitation accompanied by reflection and subsequent action. Since this Surah, as well as Surah al-Hijr, discusses monotheism (Tawhid), polytheism (Shirk), and their branches and derivatives, certainly paying attention to and acting upon their content will encompass such virtue. That is, it molds the person into its own likeness and makes them worthy of such a status and reward.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

الر كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ إِلَيْكَ لِتُخْرِجَ النَّاسَ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلى‌ صِراطِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَمِيدِ (1) اللَّهِ الَّذِي لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ وَيْلٌ لِلْكافِرِينَ مِنْ عَذابٍ شَدِيدٍ (2) الَّذِينَ يَسْتَحِبُّونَ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا عَلَى الْآخِرَةِ وَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَ يَبْغُونَها عِوَجاً أُولئِكَ فِي ضَلالٍ بَعِيدٍ (3)

Commentary

Emerging from Darkness into Light!

This Surah, like some other Surahs of the Quran, begins with the disjointed letters (Alif, Lam, Ra), the interpretation of which we explained at the beginning of Surahs al-Baqarah, Al-i-Imran, and al-A'raf. A point we find necessary to mention here is that out of the 29 instances where Surahs of the Quran begin with disjointed letters, in exactly 24 of them, the Holy Quran is mentioned immediately afterward. This indicates a connection between the two—the disjointed letters and the Quran. This connection may be what we stated at the beginning of Surah al-Baqarah: Allah wishes to clarify through this statement that this great Heavenly Book, with its magnificent content that undertakes the guidance of all humanity, is composed of simple elements called "alphabetical letters." This is a sign of the significance of this miracle—that He has brought forth the most sublime phenomenon from the simplest of phenomena!

In any case, after mentioning the letters Alif, Lam, Ra, He says: "This is a Book which We have revealed to you so that you may bring forth the people from darknesses into light" (كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ إِلَيْكَ لِتُخْرِجَ النَّاسَ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ).

In truth, all the educational, human, spiritual, and material objectives of the revelation of the Quran are gathered in this single sentence: "Bringing forth from darknesses into light!" From the darkness of ignorance to the light of knowledge, from the darkness of disbelief to the light of faith, from the darkness of tyranny and oppression to the light of justice, from the darkness of corruption to the light of righteousness, from the darkness of sin to the light of purity and piety, and from the darkness of dispersion, discord, and hypocrisy to the light of unity.

It is interesting that "darkness" (ظلمات) here—as in some other Surahs of the Quran—appears in the plural form, while "light" (نور) appears in the singular. This is an indication that all goodness, purity, faith, piety, and virtue take on a state of unity and oneness under the radiance of the light of monotheism; they are all related and united, and under that light, a single, integrated, and pure society is built in every respect.

However, darkness everywhere is the cause of the dispersion and fragmentation of ranks. Oppressors, evildoers, those tainted by sin, and the deviant often lack unity even in their deviant paths and are in a state of conflict with one another.

And since the source of all goodness is the Pure Essence of God, and the essential condition for understanding monotheism is paying attention to this very reality, He immediately adds: "All this is by the permission of their Lord (the Lord of the people)" (بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ).

Then, to further explain and clarify what is meant by this light, He says:

"Toward the path of the All-Mighty, the All-Praiseworthy" (إِلى‌ صِراطِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَمِيدِ) [^1]. Allah, whose Might (Izzat) is proof of His power, for no one has the ability to overcome Him; and His being Praiseworthy (Hamid) is a sign of His endless bounties and blessings, for praise and extolment are always in response to blessings, gifts, and beauties.

In the following verse, as a way of introducing Allah, He presents a lesson in monotheism, saying: "The same Allah to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth" (اللَّهِ الَّذِي لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ) [^2].

[^1]: "إِلى‌ صِراطِ ..." is actually an appositive (badal) for "إِلَى النُّورِ", the result of which is that the purpose of guidance toward the light is the same as guidance toward the path of the All-Mighty, the All-Praiseworthy. Additionally, "كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ" is the predicate (khabar) for an omitted subject (mubtada'), originally being "هذا كتاب انزلناه" (This is a Book We revealed).

[^2]: "اللَّهِ" with the genitive case (kasra) is an appositive (badal) for "العزيز" and "الحميد" mentioned in the previous verse.

Why does everything belong to Him? Because He is the Creator of all beings, and for this reason, He is both Powerful and Mighty (Aziz), and the Bestower of blessings and Praiseworthy (Hamid).

At the end of the verse, He draws attention to the issue of the Resurrection (after drawing attention to the Origin) and says: "Woe to the disbelievers from a severe punishment [of the Resurrection]" (وَ وَيْلٌ لِلْكافِرِينَ مِنْ عَذابٍ شَدِيدٍ).

In the next verse, He immediately introduces the disbelievers and, by mentioning three of their characteristics, clearly defines their condition so that anyone can recognize them upon the first encounter. First, He says: "They are those who prefer the lowly life of this world over the life of the Hereafter" (الَّذِينَ يَسْتَحِبُّونَ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا عَلَى الْآخِرَةِ) [^1]. Because of this mindset, they sacrifice faith, truth, justice, honor, freedom, and nobility—which are characteristics of those who love the life of the next world—for their lowly interests, lusts, and whims. Then He says: They are not content with this much; rather, in addition to their own misguidance, they strive to misguide others as well: "They bar people from the way of Allah" (وَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ).

In truth, they create various barriers and obstacles against the path of Allah, which is the path of primordial nature (fitrah) that a person can traverse on their own. They beautify whims,

[^1]: Raghib says in al-Mufradat: The meaning of "preferring disbelief over faith" is that they prioritize disbelief over faith. The reality of "istihbab" is that a person strives in the love of something, and whenever this word is used transitively with "على", it conveys the meaning of prioritizing, as in: "As for Thamud, We guided them, but they preferred blindness over guidance."

encourage people toward sin, and frighten them away from righteousness and purity.

But their work is not only creating barriers and obstacles in the way of Allah; rather, in addition to that, "they seek to make it appear crooked" (وَ يَبْغُونَها عِوجاً).

In fact, they strive with all their might to make others like themselves and of their own creed. For this reason, they try to bend the straight divine path and reach this goal by adding superstitions, various distortions, and inventing ugly and foul traditions.

It is clear that "these individuals, possessing these traits and actions, are in a far-reaching misguidance" (أُولئِكَ فِي ضَلالٍ بَعِيدٍ).

A misguidance from which their return to the path of truth is not easily possible due to the distance and remoteness, but all of this is the product of their own actions!

Key Points

1- Likening Faith and the Way of God to Light

Considering that "light" is the most subtle entity in the material world, and its speed of travel is the highest of speeds, and its blessings and effects in the material world are more than anything else—such that it can be said that the source of all material gifts and blessings is light—it becomes clear how meaningful it is to liken faith and stepping onto the path of God to it.

Light is the cause of gathering, and darkness is the factor of dispersion; light is a sign of life, and darkness is a sign of death.

For this reason, in the Holy Quran, very valuable matters are likened to light.

Among them is righteous action: يَوْمَ تَرَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَ الْمُؤْمِناتِ يَسْعى‌ نُورُهُمْ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ بِأَيْمانِهِمْ: "The Day you shall see the believing men and believing women, their light running forward before them and by their right hands" (al-Hadid, 12).

Faith and monotheism, as in: اللَّهُ وَلِيُّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يُخْرِجُهُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ: "Allah is the Guardian of those who believe; He brings them out from darknesses into the light" (al-Baqarah, 257).

The Quran is also likened to light where He says: فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِهِ وَ عَزَّرُوهُ وَ نَصَرُوهُ وَ اتَّبَعُوا النُّورَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ مَعَهُ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ: "So those who believe in him, honor him, help him, and follow the light which has been sent down with him—it is they who are the successful" (al-A'raf, 157). Also, the law of God and the divine religion are likened to this blessed entity, such as: يُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُطْفِؤُا نُورَ اللَّهِ بِأَفْواهِهِمْ ...: "They intend to extinguish the light of Allah with their mouths..." (al-Tawbah, 32).

And above all, the Pure Essence of Allah, who is the most supreme and exalted Being—rather, all of existence is a reflection of His Holy Being—is referred to as Light where we read: اللَّهُ نُورُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ: "Allah is the Light of the heavens and the earth" (al-Nur, 35).

Since all these matters return to one reality—for they are all reflections of Allah, faith in Him, His word, and His path—this word appears in these instances in the singular form. In contrast, "darknesses" (ظلمات), which is always a factor of discord and dispersion, is mentioned in the plural form, which is a sign of multiplicity and plurality.

And since faith in God and stepping onto His path is a cause for movement, a cause for awakening, a factor for assembly and unity, and a means for growth and progress, this comparison is eloquent, meaningful, and instructive from every perspective.

2- The expression "li-tukhrija" (that you may bring forth) in the first verse actually points to two notes: First, although the Holy Quran is a book of guidance and salvation for humanity, it requires an executor and implementer. There must be a leader like the Prophet who, by means of it, guides those lost on the path of truth from the darknesses of misery to the light of happiness. Therefore, even the Quran, with all its greatness, without the existence of a leader, guide, executor, and implementer, will not solve all problems.

Second, the expression "bringing forth" is actually evidence of movement accompanied by change and transformation. It is as if the people without faith are in a closed and dark atmosphere and environment, and the Prophet and leader takes their hand and brings them into a wider and brighter atmosphere.

3- It is noteworthy that the beginning of this Surah starts with the issue of guiding "the people" from darknesses to light, and its end also concludes with the issue of proclamation and warning to "the people." This shows that the primary goal in any case is the people themselves, their destiny, and their guidance. In fact, the sending of prophets and the revelation of Heavenly Books are all for the sake of reaching this very goal.

Ayahs

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا بِلِسَانِ قَوْمِهِۦ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ ۖ فَيُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٤)
We did not send any apostle except with the language of his people, so that he might make [Our messages] clear to them. Then Allah leads astray whomever He wishes, and He guides whomsoever He wishes, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (4)
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أَنْ أَخْرِجْ قَوْمَكَ مِنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ وَذَكِّرْهُم بِأَيَّىٰمِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ (٥)
Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs: ‘Bring your people out from darkness into light and remind them of Allah’s [holy] days. There are indeed signs in that for every patient and grateful [servant].’ (5)
وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ أَنجَىٰكُم مِّنْ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ وَيُذَبِّحُونَ أَبْنَآءَكُمْ وَيَسْتَحْيُونَ نِسَآءَكُمْ ۚ وَفِى ذَٰلِكُم بَلَآءٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَظِيمٌ (٦)
When Moses said to his people, ‘Remember Allah’s blessing upon you when He delivered you from Pharaoh’s clan who inflicted a terrible torment on you, and slaughtered your sons and spared your women, and in that there was a great test from your Lord. (6)
وَإِذْ تَأَذَّنَ رَبُّكُمْ لَئِن شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ ۖ وَلَئِن كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذَابِى لَشَدِيدٌ (٧)
And when your Lord proclaimed, “If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing], but if you are ungrateful, My punishment is indeed severe.” ’ (7)

Commentary

Sensitive Days of Life

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the Magnificent Quran and its life-giving effects. In the first verse under discussion, this same subject is pursued from a specific dimension: the unity of the language of the Prophets and their Heavenly Books with the language of the first people to whom they were sent.

It says: "And We did not send any messenger except with the language of his people" (وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا بِلِسَانِ قَوْمِهِ).

This is because the Prophets, in the first instance, were in contact with their own people—the nation from which they arose. The first rays of revelation shone upon them through the Prophets, and their first companions and helpers were chosen from among them. Therefore, the Prophet must speak in their language and dialect "so that he may make [the truths] clear to them" (لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ).

In fact, this phrase also alludes to the point that the call of the Prophets did not usually reflect in the hearts of their followers through some mysterious and unknown influence; rather, it took place through explanation, enlightenment, teaching, and training using the same common and prevalent language.

Then it adds that after the clarification of the Divine call to them, "Allah leaves to stray whom He wills and guides whom He wills" (فَيُضِلُّ اللَّهُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ).

This indicates that guidance and misguidance are not, in the final analysis, the work of the Prophets; their task is communication and clarification. It is Allah who holds the actual direction and guidance of His servants in His hand.

However, so that it is not imagined that the meaning of this statement is predestination, compulsion, or the negation of human freedom, He immediately adds: "And He is the All-Mighty, the All-Wise" (وَ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ). By virtue of His Might and Power, He is capable of all things, and no one has the power to resist His Will. Yet, by virtue of His Wisdom, He does not guide or misguide anyone without reason or cause. Rather, the first steps are taken by the servants with complete freedom of will on the path of journeying toward Allah, and then the light of guidance and Divine grace shines upon their hearts, just as in Surah Al-Ankabut, verse 69: وَ الَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنا: "And those who strive in Our cause, We will surely guide them to Our paths."

Likewise, those who have stripped themselves of the merit for guidance through stubbornness, prejudice, enmity toward the truth, immersion in lusts, and contamination with oppression and tyranny, are deprived of the grace of guidance and go astray in the wilderness of misguidance, just as He says:

كَذلِكَ يُضِلُّ اللَّهُ مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ مُرْتابٌ: "Thus does Allah leave to stray him who is a transgressor and a doubter" (Ghafir: 34).

And He also says: وَ ما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ: "And He misleads thereby only the defiantly disobedient" (Al-Baqarah: 26).

And He says: وَ يُضِلُّ اللَّهُ الظَّالِمِينَ: "And Allah leaves the wrongdoers to stray" (Ibrahim: 27).

In this way, the source of guidance and misguidance lies in our own hands.

In the next verse, He refers to one of the examples of sending Prophets against the tyrants of their time for the purpose of bringing them out of darkness into light, saying: "And We certainly sent Moses with Our signs (various miracles) and commanded him:

'Bring out your people from darknesses into the light' (وَ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَى بِآيَاتِنَا أَنْ أَخْرِجْ قَوْمَكَ مِنَ الظُّلُمَاتِ إِلَى النُّورِ) [1]. Just as we read in the first verse of this Surah, the program of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was also summarized as bringing people out of darknesses toward the light. This shows that this is the goal of all Prophets and Divine messengers, and indeed all spiritual leaders of humanity. For are evils, ugliness, misguidance, deviations, oppression, exploitation, humiliation, corruption, and contamination anything other than darkness? And are faith, monotheism, purity, piety, freedom, independence, honor, and dignity anything other than light and brightness? Therefore, this is precisely the common denominator and the comprehensive element among all the calls of Divine leaders.

Then He mentions one of the great missions of Moses, saying:

"And remind them of the Days of Allah" (وَ ذَكِّرْهُمْ بِأَيَّامِ اللَّهِ).

Certainly, all days are the Days of Allah, just as all places belong to God. If a specific point is called Baytullah (the House of Allah), it is due to its special significance. Similarly, the title "Days of Allah" certainly refers to specific days that possess extraordinary distinction, brightness, and brilliance.

For this reason, commentators have offered various possibilities in its interpretation:

Some have said it refers to the days of victory for previous Prophets and their truthful nations, and the days when various Divine blessings, due to their merit, encompassed

[1]: The miracles and extraordinary acts that were manifested by Moses son of Imran, referred to in the above verse with the word "signs" (ayat), were nine major miracles according to verse 101 of Surah Al-Isra, the explanation of which will come under that same verse, God willing.

them.

Some have said it refers to the days when Allah bound rebellious nations in the chains of punishment and harvested tyrants with a single command! And some have considered it to refer to both categories.

But fundamentally, one cannot limit this expressive and eloquent term. The "Days of Allah" are all days that possess greatness in the history of human life.

Every day in which one of Allah's commands shone so brightly that it overshadowed all other matters is among the Days of Allah.

Every day that opened a new chapter in human life, provided a lesson of warning, witnessed the emergence and uprising of a Prophet, or saw a headstrong tyrant or Pharaoh sent to the depths of the valley of non-existence—in short, every day in which truth and justice were established and an oppression or innovation was extinguished—all of them are among the Days of Allah.

And as we shall see, in the narrations of the Infallible Imams علیهم السلام regarding the interpretation of this verse, the finger has been placed upon sensitive days.

At the end of the verse, He says: "Indeed in that—and in all the Days of Allah—are signs for everyone who is patient and constant, and grateful" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ).

"Sabbar" and "Shakur" are both intensive forms; one conveys an abundance of patience and perseverance, and the other conveys an abundance of gratitude for blessings. This indicates that people of faith neither lose their composure and surrender to events during hardships and difficult days, nor do they fall into arrogance and negligence during days of victory and blessing. The mention of these two after the reference to the "Days of Allah" is seemingly directed at this very point.

In the next verse, He refers to one of those Days of Allah—those brilliant and fruitful days that existed in the history of the Children of Israel, the mention of which is a reminder for Muslims—saying: "And remember when Moses said to his people: 'Remember the favor of Allah upon you when He saved you from the people of Pharaoh'" (وَ إِذْ قالَ مُوسى‌ لِقَوْمِهِ اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَةَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ أَنْجاكُمْ مِنْ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ). Those same ruthless Pharaohs who "were afflicting you with the worst torment, slaughtering your sons and keeping your women alive for service and concubinage" (يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوءَ الْعَذابِ وَ يُذَبِّحُونَ أَبْناءَكُمْ وَ يَسْتَحْيُونَ نِساءَكُمْ).

"And in that was a great trial from your Lord for you" (وَ فِي ذلِكُمْ بَلاءٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ عَظِيمٌ).

What day could be more blessed than this, which cut off the evil of a self-willed, cold-hearted, and colonialist group from you—those who committed the greatest crimes against you? What crime could be greater than slaughtering your sons like animals (note that the Quran uses the term dhabh [slaughter] rather than qatl [killing]), and more importantly, your women being held as concubines in the clutches of a shameless enemy?

Not only in the case of the Children of Israel, but for all nations and peoples, the day they achieve freedom and independence and the hands of tyrants are cut off is among the Days of Allah, which they must always remember. A memory that, when attended to, protects them from reactionism and returning to the past state.

Yasumunakum is from the root sawm, which originally means to pursue something and seek it out, and it has also come to mean imposing a task upon another [1]. Therefore, the phrase يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوءَ الْعَذابِ means that they

[1]: Refer to Mufradat of Raghib, Tafsir Al-Manar (Vol. 1, p. 308), and Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi Vol. 7, p. 7.

were imposing the worst tortures and punishments upon you, the Children of Israel.

Is it a small pain to destroy the active force of a population and leave their women without guardians, as concubines in the clutches of a handful of cruel and oppressive individuals?

Furthermore, the use of the present tense verb (yasumuna) indicates that this practice continued for a long time [1]. It is also noteworthy that the slaughtering of sons and the enslavement of women and daughters is joined to "the worst torment" (su' al-adhab) with the conjunction "and" (waw), even though they are themselves instances of the worst torment. This is due to the importance of these two punishments, and it shows that the tyrannical and oppressive people of Pharaoh had other tortures and impositions upon the Children of Israel as well, but among them all, these two were the most severe and harsh.

Then He adds that you should also "remember when your Lord proclaimed: 'If you are grateful, I will surely increase you [in favor]; but if you are ungrateful, indeed, My punishment is severe'" (وَ إِذْ تَأَذَّنَ رَبُّكُمْ لَئِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ وَ لَئِنْ كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذابِي لَشَدِيدٌ) [2]. This verse may be a continuation of Moses' speech to the Children of Israel, inviting them to gratitude in the face of that salvation, victory, and abundant blessings, promising them an increase in favor, and threatening them with punishment in case of ingratitude. It may also be an independent sentence addressed to the Muslims. In any case, in terms of

[1]: Note that a similar verse with slight differences appeared in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 49.

[2]: Ta'adhdhana is from the form tafa''ul and means a proclamation with emphasis, because the if'al form of it (idhan) means to proclaim, and when it enters the tafa''ul form, addition and emphasis are derived from it.

the result, there is not much difference, for even if it is addressed to the Children of Israel, it has come in the Magnificent Quran as a constructive lesson for us.

Interestingly, regarding gratitude, He explicitly says la-azidannakum ("I will surely increase My blessing upon you"), but regarding ingratitude for blessings, He does not say "I will punish you," but only says "My punishment is severe." This difference in expression is evidence of the ultimate Grace of the Lord.

Key Points

1. Reminding of the Days of Allah

As we said in the commentary of the above verses, the annexation of "Days" to "Allah" refers to the decisive and important days in the lives of humans, which have been annexed to the name "Allah" because of their greatness, and also because a great Divine blessing encompassed a worthy nation or people, or a great and painful Divine punishment overtook a rebellious and transgressive nation. In both cases, they are worthy of mention and remembrance.

In the narrations that have reached us from the Infallible Imams علیهم السلام, the "Days of Allah" have been interpreted as various days:

In a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read that he said: "The Days of Allah are the day the Qaim عليه السلام rises, the day of the Return (al-Karrah), and the Day of Resurrection." [1] And in the commentary of Ali ibn Ibrahim, it is stated that the "Days of Allah" are three days: the day of the rising of the Mahdi عليه السلام, the day of death, and the Day of Resurrection. [2]

[1] and [2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 526.

In another hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "The Days of Allah are His favors and His trials through His afflictions, glorified be He." [1] As we have said many times, such hadiths are never evidence of exclusivity; rather, they are an explanation of some of the clear instances.

In any case, reminding of the great days (whether days of victory or difficult and grueling days) plays an effective role in the awakening and alertness of nations. It is by inspiration from this very Heavenly message that we always keep eternal the memory of the great days that have been in the history of Islam, and every year we dedicate specific days to renewing these memories, in which we return to past history and learn important lessons from it—lessons that are extraordinarily effective for our today.

Also, in our contemporary history, especially in the glorious history of the Islamic Revolution of Iran, there are extraordinary days that are living examples of the "Days of Allah," and their memory must be kept alive every year, as they are mixed with the memory of the martyrs, the warriors, the mujahideen, and the great fighters, and then inspiration should be taken from them to guard their great legacy.

On this basis, these great days must be included in the text of our school textbooks and in the education and training of our children, and the duty of "remind them" (dhakkirhum) must also be implemented for future generations.

In the Magnificent Quran, the Days of Allah are repeatedly mentioned; that is, the days of blessing and punishment have been pointed out both regarding the Children of Israel and regarding the Muslims.

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 526.

2. The Way and Custom of Tyrants

Repeatedly in the verses of the Quran, we read that the Pharaohs slaughtered the sons of the Children of Israel and kept their women alive. This was not only the work of Pharaoh and his people; rather, throughout history, the method of every colonialist has been such that they destroyed a portion of the active, aggressive, and resistant forces, and weakened another portion to employ them in the path of their own interests, for without this, the continuation of colonialism and exploitation would not be possible for them.

But it is important to know that sometimes they truly destroy the sons (like the Pharaohs), and sometimes, through afflicting them with various addictions to narcotics and alcoholic beverages and immersing them in indecency, they disable their active force and turn them into the "living dead." This is exactly what Muslims must carefully watch out for: if their young generation becomes preoccupied with various means and loses its power of faith and physical strength, they must know that captivity and slavery are certain for them.

3. Freedom: The Supreme Blessing

Interestingly, in the above verses, after mentioning the Days of Allah, the only day explicitly pointed out is the day of the salvation of the Children of Israel from the clutches of the Pharaohs (إِذْ أَنْجاكُمْ مِنْ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ), despite the fact that in the history of the Children of Israel, there were many great days when Allah granted them great blessings under the guidance of Moses. However, the mention of the "day of salvation" in the verses under discussion is evidence of the extraordinary importance of freedom and independence in the destiny of nations.

Indeed, no nation will ever manifest its genius and talent until it is freed from dependency and released from the clutches of captivity and exploitation, nor will it step onto the path of Allah, which is the path of struggle against every kind of polytheism, oppression, and injustice. For this reason, the first task of the great Divine leaders was to free captive nations from intellectual, cultural, political, and economic captivity, then work on them and implement monotheistic and human programs.

4. Gratitude Brings Increase in Blessing, and Ingratitude Causes Destruction

Undoubtedly, Allah has no need for our gratitude in exchange for the blessings He grants us. If He has commanded gratitude, that too results in another blessing for us and is a sublime educational school.

The important thing is to see what the reality of gratitude is, so that it becomes clear where its relationship with the increase of blessing lies and how it can itself be an educational factor.

The reality of gratitude is not merely verbal thanks or saying "Alhamdulillah" and the like. Rather, gratitude has three stages: the first stage is to think carefully about who the Giver of the blessing is. This attention, faith, and awareness is the first foundation of gratitude. Beyond that comes the stage of the tongue, but higher than that is the stage of action. Practical gratitude is to think correctly about the purpose for which each blessing was given to us and to spend it in its proper place; if we do not, we have committed ingratitude. As the great scholars have said: "Gratitude is the servant's spending of all that Allah the Exalted has blessed him with in that for which it was created."

Truly, why did God give us eyes? And why did He grant the blessings of hearing and speech? Was it for anything other than to see His greatness in this world, to recognize the path of life, and to step toward perfection using these means? To perceive the truth, defend it, and fight against

falsehood? If we use these great blessings of God in these paths, it is practical gratitude to Him. But if they become a means for rebellion, self-worship, arrogance, negligence, alienation, and distance from God, this is the very essence of ingratitude. Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says:

"The lowest degree of gratitude is seeing the blessing as being from Allah without the heart becoming attached to it instead of Allah, being satisfied with what He has given, and that you do not disobey Him using His blessing or oppose Him in any of His commands or prohibitions by means of His blessing." [1]

From here, it becomes clear how the gratitude for power, knowledge, intellectual strength, social influence, wealth, and health is performed, and how their ingratitude occurs.

The hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn is also clear evidence for this interpretation, where he says: "Gratitude for the blessing is the avoidance of the forbidden." [2]

Also from here, the relationship between "gratitude" and "increase of blessing" becomes clear, because whenever humans spend God's blessings precisely for the actual goals of the blessing, they have practically proven that they are worthy and capable, and this capability and merit becomes the cause for more grace and further bounty.

Fundamentally, we have two types of gratitude: "ontological gratitude" (shukr takwini) and "legislative gratitude" (shukr tashri'i).

[1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 710. [2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 529.

"Ontological gratitude" is when a being uses the bounties at its disposal for its growth and development. For example, a gardener sees that in a certain part of the garden, the trees are growing and developing well, and the more he cares for them, the more they flourish. This very fact causes the gardener to devote more effort to training that part of the garden and to recommend their care to his workers, because those trees are crying out with the "language of their state": "O gardener! We are capable, we are worthy; increase your favor upon us!" And he responds positively to this call.

But in another part of the garden, he sees trees that have withered—"neither freshness, nor leaf, nor flower; they have neither shade nor fruit." This ingratitude for the blessing causes the gardener to treat them with disfavor, and if this situation continues, he orders the saw to be placed at their base, for: "The wood of fruitless trees should be burned; such is the proper reward for fruitlessness."

In the world of humanity, this same state exists, with the difference that a tree has no will of its own and is merely subject to ontological laws, whereas humans, by using the power of will and choice and legislative education, can consciously step onto this path.

Therefore, he who makes the blessing of power a means for oppression and rebellion is crying out with the language of his state: "O Lord, I am not worthy of this blessing!" And he who utilizes it in the path of implementing truth and justice says with the language of his state: "O Lord, I am worthy; increase it!"

This reality is also beyond doubt: whenever we set out to perform Divine gratitude—whether with thought, tongue, or action—this very ability to give thanks is itself a new bounty at every stage. In this way, the act of gratitude makes us indebted to His new blessings, and

thus we are never able to fulfill the right of His gratitude, just as we read in the "Supplication of the Grateful" (Munajat al-Shakirin) from the Fifteen Whispered Prayers of Imam al-Sajjad عليه السلام:

"How can I achieve gratitude when my thanking You requires a [new] thank? For every time I say to You 'Praise be to You,' it becomes necessary for me because of that to say to You 'Praise be to You'!"

Therefore, the highest stage of gratitude of which man is capable is to express his helplessness and inability to give thanks for His blessings, just as we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said:

"Among what Allah, Mighty and Majestic, revealed to Moses was: 'Thank Me with the gratitude due to Me.' He said: 'O Lord! And how can I thank You with the gratitude due to You, when there is no gratitude I offer You except that You have blessed me with it?' He said: 'O Moses! Now you have thanked Me, since you have known that that is from Me.'" [1]

The servant is best off bringing his apology For his shortcomings to the threshold of God, Otherwise, what is worthy of His Lordship, No one is able to perform!

A Few Important Points Regarding Gratitude for Blessings

  1. Ali عليه السلام says in one of his wise sayings in Nahj al-Balagha: "When the fringes of blessings reach you, do not repel their furthest reaches by lack of gratitude!" [1]

  1. This point is also noteworthy: merely thanking and praising God for blessings is not enough; rather, one must also thank and praise those who were the means of that bounty, fulfilling the right of their efforts through this and encouraging them toward more service. He will reply: 'O Lord, I thanked You.' He will say: 'Since you did not thank him, you have not thanked Me!'" Then the Imam said: "The most grateful of you to Allah are those who are most grateful to people." [2]

  2. The increase in Allah's blessings promised to the grateful is not only that He grants them new material blessings; rather, the act of gratitude itself—which is accompanied by special attention to God and a new love for His Holy Presence—is itself a great spiritual blessing that is extraordinarily effective in training human souls and inviting them to obey Divine commands. Indeed, gratitude is inherently a way to know God more and more, and for this reason, scholars of theology (kalam) have approached the proof of the "obligation of knowing God" (wujub ma'rifat Allah) through the obligation of "thanking the Benefactor" (shukr al-mun'im).

  3. Reviving the spirit of gratitude in society and honoring, appreciating, and thanking those

[2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 81, Hadith 30.

who have served through their knowledge, or through sacrifice and martyrdom, or through other struggles in the path of advancing social goals, is an important factor for the movement, flourishing, and dynamism of society.

In a society where the spirit of thanks and appreciation has died, few people find the interest or heart to serve. Conversely, those who show more appreciation for the efforts and services of individuals are more vibrant and progressive nations.

Attention to this very reality has caused people in our era to hold ceremonies to honor the efforts of great figures of the past on their hundredth year, thousandth year, birthday, or any other suitable opportunity, and while thanking them for their services, invite the people to more movement and effort.

For example, in the Islamic Revolution of our country, which was the end of a dark period of two thousand five hundred years and the beginning of a new era, when we see that every year, every month, and indeed every day, the memory of the martyrs of the revolution is kept alive, blessings are sent upon them, all those related to them are respected, and their services are honored—this itself causes the love and interest for sacrifice to grow in others and raises the level of the people's sacrifice. In the words of the Quran, gratitude for this blessing will cause its increase, and from the blood of one martyr, thousands of mujahideen grow, becoming a living example of la-azidannakum!
