Commentary

Is There Any Doubt About Allah?

The first verse under discussion serves as a confirmation and completion of the discourse on gratitude and ingratitude mentioned in the previous verse, related through the words of Musa bin 'Imran (peace be upon him). It states: "Musa reminded the Children of Israel that if you and all the people on earth become disbelievers (and show ingratitude for Allah's blessings)," you shall cause Him no harm, for He is Self-Sufficient and Praiseworthy. "And Musa said: 'If you disbelieve, you and whoever is on the earth altogether, then verily Allah is Self-Sufficient, Praiseworthy.'" (14:8) [1] In reality, gratitude for blessings and faith in Allah are means for the increase of your own blessings and for your own perfection and honor; otherwise, Allah is so Self-Sufficient that if all created beings were to disbelieve, not a speck of dust would settle upon the robe of His Grandeur, because He is independent of all, and does not even need thanks or praise, as He is inherently Praiseworthy (Hamid).

If any need could find its way into His Holy Essence, He would not be the "Necessary Being" (Wajib al-Wujud). Therefore, the concept of His being "Self-Sufficient" (Ghani) means that all perfections are gathered in Him, and One who is such is inherently praised, for the meaning of Hamid is nothing other than one who is worthy of Hamd (praise).

[1] It is clear that the result (jaza) of the conditional clause "If you disbelieve" is omitted, and the phrase "then verily Allah is Self-Sufficient, Praiseworthy" points to it. The implied meaning is: "If you disbelieve... you will not harm Allah in the least..."

Then, over several verses, it addresses the fate of groups from past nations—those who chose the path of ingratitude toward Divine blessings and rose in opposition and disbelief against the call of Divine leaders. It explains their logic and their ultimate end to emphasize what was said in the previous verse. It asks: "Has there not reached you the news of those before you?" (أَ لَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَبَؤُا الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ‌).

This sentence might be a continuation of Musa's speech mentioned in the previous verse, or it could be an independent statement from the Quran addressed to the Muslims; the result remains much the same. It then adds: "The people of Nuh, and 'Ad, and Thamud, and those after them" (قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَ عادٍ وَ ثَمُودَ وَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ‌).

"None knows them but Allah" (لا يَعْلَمُهُمْ إِلَّا اللَّهُ‌) [1]. Undoubtedly, some news of the people of Nuh, 'Ad, and Thamud, as well as the nations after them, has reached us, but certainly, a much larger portion has not reached us, of which only Allah is aware. There were so many secrets, characteristics, and details in the histories of past nations that perhaps what has reached us is very small and insignificant compared to what has not.

Then, as an explanation regarding their story, it says: "Their messengers came to them with clear proofs, but they put their hands to their mouths [in surprise and denial] and said: 'Verily, we disbelieve in that with which you have been sent'" (جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَدَرُّوا أَيْدِيَهُمْ فِي أَفْواهِهِمْ وَ قالُوا إِنَّا كَفَرْنا بِما أُرْسِلْتُمْ بِهِ‌).

[1] The phrase "None knows them but Allah" may be coordinated with the previous sentence with an omitted "and," or it may function as an adjectival clause for the preceding phrase.

For "we are in disquieting doubt concerning that to which you invite us" (وَ إِنَّا لَفِي شَكٍّ مِمَّا تَدْعُونَنا إِلَيْهِ مُرِيبٍ‌). With such doubt and uncertainty, how is it possible for us to accept your call?

Here, a question arises: they first expressed disbelief and lack of faith toward the prophets, but followed it by stating they were in doubt, completing it with the word murib (disquieting). How are these two consistent?

The answer is that the expression of doubt is, in fact, the reason for their lack of faith, because coming to faith requires certainty, and doubt is an obstacle to it.

Since the previous verse expressed the speech of the polytheists and disbelievers regarding their lack of faith—which they attributed to doubt and uncertainty—the next verse immediately negates their doubt with a clear proof presented in a brief phrase, saying:

"Their messengers said: 'Is there any doubt about Allah, the Creator of the heavens and the earth?'" (قالَتْ رُسُلُهُمْ أَ فِي اللَّهِ شَكٌّ فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌). Although Fatir originally means "cleaver" or "splitter," here it is an allusion to "Creator"—a Creator who creates something according to a calculated plan and then preserves and maintains it. It is as if the darkness of non-existence is split asunder by the light of existence through the blessing of His being, just as the dawn breaks through the dark veil of night, and just as a date blossom splits its sheath and the cluster of dates emerges from it (hence the Arabs call it fatr).

There is also the possibility that Fatir refers to the splitting of the primary mass of universal matter, as we read in modern sciences that the total matter of the universe was a single joined unit, which then split apart and the celestial bodies appeared.

In any case, the Quran here, as in most other instances, relies on the system of the universe and the creation of the heavens and the earth to prove the existence and attributes of Allah. We know that in the matter of knowing God (theology), no proof is more vivid or clearer than this.

For every corner of this wondrous system is filled with secrets that cry out in their very state: none but a Wise, Powerful, and All-Knowing Being has the power for such design. For this reason, the more human science and knowledge progress, the more proofs from this system are revealed, bringing us closer to Allah every moment.

Truly, what wonders the Quran holds! It has alluded to the entire discussion of theology and Monotheism (Tawhid) in this single sentence mentioned as a rhetorical question: "Is there any doubt about Allah, the Creator of the heavens and the earth?"—a sentence for which thousands of books would not suffice for its analysis and extensive discussion.

It is noteworthy that studying the secrets of existence and the system of creation does not only guide us to the principle of Allah's existence; rather, His attributes such as knowledge, power, wisdom, eternity, and everlastingness are also clarified through this study.

Then it addresses the second objection of the deniers, which is the objection to the issue of the messengers' mission (since they doubted both the principle of knowing God and the call of the Prophet), stating: It is certain that the All-Knowing and Wise Creator would never leave His servants without a leader; rather, "He invites you [by sending messengers] so that He may cleanse you of sins and impurities and forgive your sins" (يَدْعُوكُمْ لِيَغْفِرَ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ‌) [1].

[1] There is discussion among commentators regarding the meaning of min (from/of) in the phrase "so that He may forgive you of (min) your sins." Some have taken it to mean "partitive" (tab'id), meaning He forgives some of your sins. However, this possibility is very unlikely given that coming to faith causes the forgiveness of all sins ("Islam wipes away what preceded it"). Some have suggested that min is for "substitution" (badaliyyah), according to which the meaning is: Allah invites you to forgive your sins in exchange for your faith. Others have said min here is extra (za'idah) and for emphasis, meaning Allah invites you to faith to forgive all your sins. This interpretation seems the most correct of all.

And furthermore, "and give you respite until a specified term" (وَ يُؤَخِّرَكُمْ إِلى‌ أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى‌) so that you may traverse your path of perfection and derive the maximum necessary benefit from this life.

In reality, the call of the prophets was for two goals: one, the forgiveness of sins—or in other words, the purification of the soul, body, and human living environment—and the other, the continuation of life until the appointed time. These two are actually cause and effect of one another, for a society can continue its life only if it is pure from sin and oppression.

Throughout history, there have been many societies that, due to oppression, tyranny, hedonism, and various sins, died prematurely, so to speak, and did not reach the "specified term" (ajal musamma) in Quranic terminology.

In a comprehensive and interesting Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Those who die due to sins are more than those who die due to natural terms (ajal), and those who live due to righteousness are more than those who live due to [natural] lifespans." [1]

It is also narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "Verily, a man commits a sin and is thus deprived of the night prayer (Salat al-Layl); indeed, an evil deed acts faster upon its doer than a knife in meat." [1]

[1] Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 488.

Furthermore, it is clearly understood from this verse that faith in the call of the prophets and acting upon their programs prevents the "suspended term" (ajal mu'allaq) and extends human life until the "specified term" (ajal musamma). (As we know, humans have two types of terms: one is the final deadline of life, which is the ultimate capacity of the body for life, and the other is the suspended term, which is the end of a person's life due to factors and obstacles midway, often due to their own reckless actions and contamination with various sins, a topic we discussed under verse 2 of Surah Al-An'am).

Yet, despite all this, the stubborn disbelievers did not accept this life-giving call, which was infused with the clear logic of Monotheism. With a statement reeking of stubbornness and refusal to submit to the truth, they replied to their prophets: "You are nothing but humans like us!" (قالُوا إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُنا).

Additionally, "You wish to turn us away from what our fathers used to worship" (تُرِيدُونَ أَنْ تَصُدُّونا عَمَّا كانَ يَعْبُدُ آباؤُنا).

Beyond all this, "So bring us a clear authority [proof]" (فَأْتُونا بِسُلْطانٍ مُبِينٍ‌).

However, we have said many times (and the Quran has explicitly stated) that the prophets being human was not only not an obstacle to their prophethood, but rather completed it. Those who took this as a reason to deny the messengers' mission were mostly seeking excuses.

Likewise, relying on the ways and customs of ancestors—considering the fact that usually the knowledge of later generations exceeds that of the predecessors—can be nothing but blind prejudice and worthless superstition.

From this, it becomes clear that their demand for a clear proof was not because the prophets lacked it; rather, we repeatedly read in the verses of the Quran that the excuse-seekers denied clear proofs and "clear authority," and every time they would propose a new miracle or proof just to find a way to escape.

In any case, in the coming verses, we shall read how the prophets responded to them.

Ayahs

قَالَتْ لَهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ إِن نَّحْنُ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَمُنُّ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ وَمَا كَانَ لَنَآ أَن نَّأْتِيَكُم بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (١١)
Their apostles said to them, ‘Indeed we are just human beings like yourselves; but Allah favours whomever of His servants that He wishes. We may not bring you an authority except by Allah’s leave, and in Allah let all the faithful put their trust. (11)
وَمَا لَنَآ أَلَّا نَتَوَكَّلَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ وَقَدْ هَدَىٰنَا سُبُلَنَا ۚ وَلَنَصْبِرَنَّ عَلَىٰ مَآ ءَاذَيْتُمُونَا ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ (١٢)
And why should we not put our trust in Allah, seeing that He has guided us in our ways? Surely, we will put up patiently with whatever torment you may inflict upon us, and in Allah let all the trusting put their trust.’ (12)

Commentary

Rely Only on Allah

In these two verses, we read the response of the Prophets to the excuses of the stubborn opponents mentioned in the previous verses.

In response to their objection that "Why are you from the human race?", "The Prophets said to them in reply: Indeed, we are only humans like you, but Allah bestows His favor upon whom He wills of His servants and grants them the gift of messengership"

(قالَتْ لَهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ إِنْ نَحْنُ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ وَ لكِنَّ اللَّهَ يَمُنُّ عَلى‌ مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ‌).

That is to say: Do not forget that if, hypothetically, an angel had been chosen instead of a human, the angel would still have nothing of its own; all blessings, including the gift of messengership and leadership, are from Allah. He who can grant such a status to an angel can also grant it to a human.

It is evident that the granting of this gift by Allah is not arbitrary, and we have stated many times that Allah's Will is harmonized with His Wisdom. Thus, wherever we read:

"Allah [bestows] upon whom He wills..." the meaning is "whomever He deems worthy." It is true that the station of messengership is ultimately a Divine gift, but certain predispositions must certainly exist within the person of the Prophet as well.

Then, the verse addresses the answer to the third question without responding to the second objection—as if their second objection regarding following the traditions of their ancestors was so weak and baseless that any rational person could understand the answer with the slightest reflection. Furthermore, the answer to this claim has been given in other verses of the Quran.

Indeed, in response to the third question, it says that bringing miracles is not our personal work, such that we sit in a corner like a performer of extraordinary feats and everyone proposes a miracle according to their own whim, turning the matter of breaking natural laws into a worthless plaything. Rather: "It is not for us to bring you a miracle except by the permission of Allah" (وَ ما كانَ لَنا أَنْ نَأْتِيَكُمْ بِسُلْطانٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ‌).

Moreover, every Prophet demonstrates sufficient miracles even without the people's request to serve as proof of his truthfulness, although studying the content of their call and school of thought is itself the greatest miracle. However, the excuse-seekers often did not pay attention to such words; they would propose something new every day, and if the Prophet did not submit, they would raise a hue and cry.

Then, to give a decisive response to the various threats of these excuse-seekers, they defined their position with this sentence, saying: "And upon Allah let the believers rely"—the same Allah before whose power all other powers are insignificant and worthless (وَ عَلَى اللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

Then, they proceeded with a clear argument for this matter of reliance (Tawakkul), saying: "Why should we not rely upon 'Allah' and seek refuge in Him in all difficulties? Why should we fear hollow powers and threats while He has guided us to the paths of our happiness?" (وَ ما لَنا أَلَّا نَتَوَكَّلَ عَلَى اللَّهِ وَ قَدْ هَدانا سُبُلَنا).

When He has granted us the greatest blessing—the blessing of guidance to the paths of happiness—He will certainly place us under His protection against any aggression, sabotage, or problem.

And then they continued: Now that our reliance is upon Allah—an invincible support that is above all things—"We will surely be patient and steadfast against all the harm you inflict upon us" (وَ لَنَصْبِرَنَّ عَلى‌ ما آذَيْتُمُونا).

And finally, they concluded their speech with these words: "And upon Allah let all those who rely, put their trust" (وَ عَلَى اللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ‌).

Key Points

1- The Reliance of the Believers and the Reliance of the Trusting:

1- In the first verse under discussion, we read that "the believers" must rely on Allah, and in the second verse, we read that "the trusting" (al-mutawakkilun) must rely on Allah. It seems as if the second phrase is a broader and higher stage than the first. That is to say, not only the believers—since faith in Allah cannot be separated from faith in His power and support and reliance upon Him—but even non-believers and everyone else can find no support other than Allah. For whomever they look upon has nothing of their own; all blessings, powers, and gifts return to His Pure Essence. Therefore, they too must lay their heads upon His threshold and ask of Him, and this reliance also invites them toward faith in Allah.

2- A Clear Answer to Those Who Deny the Miracles of the Prophets:

2- The above verses provide a clear answer to those who deny the miracles of the Prophets, or deny the miracles of the Prophet of Islam other than the Quran. It makes us understand that the Prophets never said they would not bring miracles; rather, they said they would not do so except by the command and permission of Allah, because miracles are His work and under His control, and He grants them whenever He deems it appropriate.

3- The Reality of Tawakkul and Its Philosophy:

"Tawakkul" (reliance/trust) originally comes from the root "wakalat," meaning to appoint a representative (wakil). We know that a good representative is someone who possesses at least four qualities: sufficient knowledge, trustworthiness, power, and compassion.

It perhaps does not need mentioning that choosing a representative to defend one's affairs occurs when a person is personally unable to defend themselves. At such a time, one utilizes another power and solves their problem with that help.

Therefore, relying on Allah means nothing other than that a person, when faced with the difficulties and events of life, the enmities and stubbornness of opponents, and the complexities and occasional dead-ends they encounter on the path toward their goal—where they lack the power to resolve them—makes Him their representative and relies on Him. This does not mean ceasing effort and striving; rather, even where one has the ability to perform a task, they still consider Allah the primary cause, for through the eyes of a monotheist, He is the source of all powers and forces.

The opposite of "reliance on Allah" is relying on other than Him—that is, living in a state of dependency, being attached to others, and lacking independence. Ethicists say: Tawakkul is the direct fruit of "Monotheism in Actions" (Tawhid al-Af'ali), because as we said, from the perspective of a monotheist, every movement, effort, struggle, and every phenomenon that occurs in the world is ultimately linked to the First Cause of this world, namely the Essence of Allah. Therefore, a monotheist attributes all powers and victories to Him.

The Philosophy of Tawakkul

Based on what has been mentioned, it is concluded that:

Firstly: Reliance on Allah—on that inexhaustible source of power and ability—causes an increase in human resistance against the difficulties and harsh events of life. For this reason, when the Muslims suffered a heavy blow at the field of "Uhud," and the enemies, after leaving the field, turned back halfway to deliver a final blow to the Muslims, and this news reached the ears of the believers, the Quran says that the faithful individuals—not only in this very dangerous moment when they had lost a major part of their active force—did not panic. Rather, by leaning on "Tawakkul" and seeking help from the power of faith, their steadfastness increased, and the victorious enemy, upon hearing news of this readiness, retreated quickly (Al-Imran, verse 173).

Examples of this steadfastness in the shadow of Tawakkul are seen in numerous verses. For instance, in verse 122 of Al-Imran, the Quran says: "Reliance on Allah prevented the weakness of two groups of warriors in the field of Jihad."

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 10; p. 296

In verse 12 of Surah Ibrahim, "Tawakkul is mentioned as being inseparable from patience and endurance against the attacks and injuries of the enemy."

And in verse 159 of Al-Imran, "For performing important tasks, the command is given first to consult, then to make a firm decision, and then to rely on Allah."

The Quran even says: against satanic whisperings, "Only those can resist and escape his influence who possess faith and Tawakkul" (إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ لَهُ سُلْطانٌ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَلى‌ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ - An-Nahl 99).

From the sum of these verses, it is understood that the purpose of Tawakkul is that a person should not feel insignificant or weak in the face of the magnitude of problems, but rather, by relying on the infinite power of Allah, should consider themselves victorious and triumphant. In this way, Tawakkul is hope-inspiring, empowering, and strengthening, and a cause for increased endurance and resistance.

If the concept of Tawakkul meant crawling into a corner and sitting idle, it would make no sense for it to be applied to warriors and the like.

And if some imagine that paying attention to the world of causes and natural factors is incompatible with the spirit of Tawakkul, they are gravely mistaken. For separating the effects of natural factors from the Will of Allah is considered a type of polytheism (Shirk). Is it not true that natural factors also have whatever they possess from Him, and all are by His Will and command? Yes, if we consider the factors as an independent system against His Will, that is where it would be incompatible with the spirit of Tawakkul. (Reflect carefully).

How could such an interpretation be given for Tawakkul when the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله himself, who was the leader of those who rely on Allah, never neglected any opportunity, correct planning, positive tactics, or various external means and causes to advance his goals? All of this proves that Tawakkul does not have that negative meaning.

Secondly: Reliance on Allah saves a person from dependencies that are the source of humiliation and slavery, and grants them freedom and self-confidence.

"Tawakkul" (reliance) and "Qana'at" (contentment) share common roots, and naturally, their philosophies are similar in some respects, while also having differences. Here we present a few Islamic narrations regarding Tawakkul—as a light upon its original concept and root:

Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says:

ان الغناء و العز يجولان فاذا ظفرا بموضع التوكل اوطنا

[^1] "Self-sufficiency and honor are in motion; when they find the place of Tawakkul, they take up residence there."

In this Hadith, the original home of self-sufficiency and honor is identified as "Tawakkul."

It is narrated from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: I asked the messenger of Allah's revelation, Gabriel, what is Tawakkul? He said:

العلم بان المخلوق لا يضر و لا ينفع، و لا يعطى و لا يمنع، و استعمال الياس من الخلق فاذا كان العبد كذلك لم يعمل لاحد سوى اللَّه و لم يطمع فى احد سوى اللَّه فهذا هو التوكل‌

[^2] "The knowledge that the creature neither harms nor benefits, neither gives nor withholds, and practicing detachment from the creation. When a servant becomes like this, he works for no one but Allah and hopes in no one but Allah; this is the reality of Tawakkul."

Someone asked Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام:

ما حد التوكل؟ فقال ان لا تخاف مع اللَّه احدا [^3]

"What is the limit of Tawakkul? He replied: That you fear no one while relying on Allah!" [^4]

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Volume 2, Chapter on Delegation to Allah and Reliance upon Him, Hadith 3.

[^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 15, Part 2, On Ethics, p. 14, old edition.

[^3]: Safinat al-Bihar, Volume 2, page 682.

[^4]: For further explanation regarding Tawakkul and the like, refer to the book "The Motive for the Emergence of Religion" (Angizeh-ye Peydayesh-e Mazhab).

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِرُسُلِهِمْ لَنُخْرِجَنَّكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِنَآ أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِى مِلَّتِنَا ۖ فَأَوْحَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ لَنُهْلِكَنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٣)
But the faithless said to their apostles, ‘Surely we will expel you from our land, or you shall revert to our creed.’ Thereat their Lord revealed to them: ‘We will surely destroy the wrongdoers, (13)
وَلَنُسْكِنَنَّكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَنْ خَافَ مَقَامِى وَخَافَ وَعِيدِ (١٤)
and surely We will settle you in the land after them. This [promise] is for someone who is awed to stand before Me and fears My threat.’ (14)
وَٱسْتَفْتَحُوا۟ وَخَابَ كُلُّ جَبَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ (١٥)
They prayed for victory [against the infidels], and every obstinate tyrant was defeated, (15)
مِّن وَرَآئِهِۦ جَهَنَّمُ وَيُسْقَىٰ مِن مَّآءٍ صَدِيدٍ (١٦)
with hell lying ahead of him, [where] he shall be given to drink of a purulent fluid, (16)
يَتَجَرَّعُهُۥ وَلَا يَكَادُ يُسِيغُهُۥ وَيَأْتِيهِ ٱلْمَوْتُ مِن كُلِّ مَكَانٍ وَمَا هُوَ بِمَيِّتٍ ۖ وَمِن وَرَآئِهِۦ عَذَابٌ غَلِيظٌ (١٧)
gulping it down, but hardly swallowing it: death will assail him from every side, but he will not die, and there is [yet] a harsh punishment ahead of him. (17)

Commentary

The Program and Fate of Stubborn Tyrants

As is the way of irrational individuals, when they become aware of the weakness and impotence of their own speech and beliefs, they abandon reasoning and rely on force, power, and bullying. Here too, we read that the stubborn and excuse-seeking disbelieving nations, upon hearing the firm and eloquent logic of the Prophets mentioned in the previous verses, said to their Prophets: "We swear that we will expel you from our land, unless you return to our creed—idolatry!" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِرُسُلِهِمْ لَنُخْرِجَنَّكُمْ مِنْ أَرْضِنا أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِي مِلَّتِنا).

These oblivious, arrogant people spoke as if they owned all the lands and did not grant their Prophets even the rights of a citizen; hence they said: "Our land" (أَرْضِنا), whereas Allah created the earth and all its blessings for the righteous. In reality, these self-centered, arrogant tyrants have no right to it, let alone claiming it all as their own!

The phrase "لَتَعُودُنَّ فِي مِلَّتِنا" (you must return to our creed) might create the misconception that the Prophets followed the creed of idolatry before their mission. However, this is not the case. Regardless of the issue of infallibility ('ismah), which includes the period before prophethood, their intellect and wisdom were far too great to commit such an irrational act as prostrating before stones and wood.

This expression might be used because the Prophets did not have a mission to preach before their formal appointment; perhaps their silence created the illusion among the polytheists that they shared the same beliefs.

Furthermore, although the address is outwardly to the Prophets themselves, it actually includes their followers. We know that these followers were previously on the creed of the polytheists, and the polytheists' view was primarily directed at them. The general expression "لتعودن" (you all shall return) is used by way of taghlib (applying the rule of the majority to the whole). [^1] Then it adds that at this moment, Allah comforted the Prophets and reassured them, "and sent revelation to them that: I will certainly destroy the wrongdoers and oppressors" (فَأَوْحى‌ إِلَيْهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ لَنُهْلِكَنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ‌).

Therefore, never fear these threats, and let not the slightest weakness enter your iron will.

Since the oppressive deniers threatened the Prophets with exile from their land, Allah promises them in return: "I will settle you in this land after their destruction" (وَ لَنُسْكِنَنَّكُمُ الْأَرْضَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ‌). However, this victory and success are not granted to everyone; "it is for those who fear My station and feel a sense of responsibility, and are also fearful of the threat of punishments for deviation, oppression, and sin, taking them seriously" (ذلِكَ لِمَنْ خافَ مَقامِي وَ خافَ وَعِيدِ).

Thus, My blessing and grace are neither random nor without reason; rather, they are reserved for those who, with a sense of responsibility before the station of the Lord's justice, neither approach oppression nor show stubbornness and enmity toward the call of Truth.

At the point when the Prophets were pushed to their limit and had fulfilled their entire duty toward their people—those who were meant to believe had believed, and the rest persisted in disbelief and repeatedly threatened the Messengers—at this moment, "they sought victory and opening from Allah" (وَ اسْتَفْتَحُوا). And Allah answered the prayer of these true mujahideen such that "every stubborn tyrant was disappointed, lost, and destroyed" (وَ خابَ كُلُّ جَبَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ).

"خابَ" is derived from the root "khaybah" (rhyming with ghaybah), meaning the loss of what is desired, which is nearly equivalent to "disappointment" or "hopelessness" in English.

"جَبَّارٍ" (Jabbar) here means arrogant and rebellious. In a hadith, it is mentioned that a woman came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; he gave her a command, but she disobeyed and did not execute it. The Prophet said: "Leave her, for she is a rebellious (jabbara) woman." [^1] However, the word "Jabbar" is sometimes applied to Allah as well, where it carries a different meaning: "The One who mends the condition of those in need of reform" or "The One who has absolute mastery over all things." [^2]

[^1]: A different answer has been given to this misconception: the root "‘awd" (return), if made transitive with the preposition "ila", means "to return," but if made transitive with "fi", it means "to change state or undergo a transformation" and does not imply returning. Thus, the sentence "لَتَعُودُنَّ فِي مِلَّتِنا" would mean "you must change, abandon your creed, and accept ours." This meaning was chosen by Allamah Tabataba'i in Al-Mizan. However, referring to verses such as "كُلَّما أَرادُوا أَنْ يَخْرُجُوا مِنْها أُعِيدُوا فِيها" (Sajdah: 20) and some other Quranic verses shows that "‘awd" means "return" even when used with the word "fi". (Observe closely).

[^1]: Quoted from the Tafsir of Fakhr al-Razi under the verse. [^2]: For further explanation, refer to Volume 4 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 338.

The word "عَنِيدٍ" (Anid) originally comes from "‘ind", meaning side or direction, and here it means deviation and leaning away from the path of Truth.

Hence, we read in a narration from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "Every stubborn tyrant (jabbar ‘anid) is one who refuses to say: There is no god but Allah." [^1] And in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "The ‘anid is the one who turns away from the Truth." [^2] It is interesting that "Jabbar" refers to an internal psychological trait—the rebellious spirit—while "Anid" refers to the effect of that trait on human actions, which deviates the person from the Truth.

Then, regarding the outcome of these stubborn tyrants in terms of the punishments of the next world, He points to five subjects within two verses:

1- "Following this disappointment and loss—or following such a person—will be Hell and the blazing fire" (مِنْ وَرائِهِ جَهَنَّمُ‌).

The word "وراء" (wara'), although it means "behind" (the opposite of "in front"), is used in such contexts to mean the result and consequence of an action. This is similar to common expressions where we say: "If you eat such food, illness is behind it," or "If you befriend such a person, misery and regret follow," meaning that is the result and effect.

2- "In the midst of that blazing fire, when he becomes thirsty, he is given 'sadid' water to drink" (وَ يُسْقى‌ مِنْ ماءٍ صَدِيدٍ).

[^1] and [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 532.

"صَدِيدٍ" (Sadid), as linguists have stated, is the pus that collects between the skin and the flesh, indicating that he is given a foul-smelling, putrid, and hideous liquid like pus and blood to drink.

3- When this sinful criminal and stubborn tyrant finds himself before such a drink, "he gulps it down with difficulty, and is never willing to drink it," rather it is forced down his throat (يَتَجَرَّعُهُ وَ لا يَكادُ يُسِيغُهُ‌). [^1]

4- So many means of punishment, torture, and distress are provided for him that "death comes to him from every side, yet despite all this, he never dies," so that he may taste the punishment for his evil deeds (وَ يَأْتِيهِ الْمَوْتُ مِنْ كُلِّ مَكانٍ وَ ما هُوَ بِمَيِّتٍ‌).

And while one might imagine there is no punishment greater than those mentioned, He adds: "And behind it is a harsh punishment" (وَ مِنْ وَرائِهِ عَذابٌ غَلِيظٌ).

Thus, whatever intensity of punishment and penalty the human mind can conceive—and even what it cannot—awaits these self-centered oppressors and faithless sinners. Their bed is of fire, their drink is putrid and repulsive, various punishments come from every side and every direction, and yet there is no dying, but rather living and tasting again! However, one must never imagine that such punishments are unjust, for as we have said many times, these are the natural results and effects of human actions themselves. Rather, they are the embodiment of their deeds in the other realm, where every action is manifested in its appropriate form. If we carefully consider the actions and crimes of certain criminals we have witnessed in our own era or read about in history, we sometimes think that even these punishments are too little for them! [^1]

Key Points

1- What is meant by the "Station of the Lord"?

In the verses above, we read that victory over the oppressors and governance of the land following their destruction belongs to those who fear the "Station of Allah" (مقام خداوند). Regarding what is meant by the word "Station" (Maqam) here, several possibilities have been suggested, all of which may be correct and intended by the verse:

A: It refers to the position of the Lord during the Reckoning, as we read in other verses of the Quran: "وَ أَمَّا مَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ وَ نَهَى النَّفْسَ عَنِ الْهَوى‌ ..." (Naziat: 40) and "وَ لِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ جَنَّتانِ‌" (Rahman: 46).

B: "Station" (Maqam) means "Qiyam" (standing), and Qiyam means "supervision and watchfulness," meaning the one who is fearful of Allah's intense supervision over his actions and feels a sense of responsibility.

C: "Station" means "standing for the execution of justice and the restoration of rights," meaning those who fear this position of the Lord.

[^1]: Yusighuhu is from the root "isaghah", meaning to pour a drink down the throat. [^1]: In this very devastating war that we are facing while writing this discussion—which we know is nothing but the product of the selfishness of an oppressive ruler, or more accurately, a mad, stubborn tyrant, for which no rational goal can be imagined—what crimes and oppressions have occurred that the tongue and pen are unable to describe! We ourselves saw examples of the war-wounded in the hospitals of the west of the country, from innocent children to elderly men and women who had lost their eyes or limbs and were in a state that would truly shake any human being. Think: when a tyrant afflicts thousands upon thousands of people in this way, what punishment can be envisioned for him?!

In any case, as we said, there is no obstacle to all these meanings being combined in the concept of the verse. That is, those who see God as watchful over them are apprehensive and fearful of His reckoning and execution of justice—a constructive fear that invites them to a sense of responsibility in every task and restrains them from any form of injustice, oppression, and contamination with sin. Victory and governance on earth ultimately belong to them.

2- There is a discussion among commentators regarding the interpretation of the phrase "وَ اسْتَفْتَحُوا". ** Some have taken it to mean "requesting victory and opening," as we mentioned above. The evidence for this is verse 19 of Surah Al-Anfal: "إِنْ تَسْتَفْتِحُوا فَقَدْ جاءَكُمُ الْفَتْحُ‌**" (If you [O believers] seek victory, then victory has already come to you).

Others have interpreted it as "requesting judgment and governance," meaning the Prophets asked God to judge between them and the disbelievers. The evidence for this is verse 89 of Surah Al-A'raf: "رَبَّنَا افْتَحْ بَيْنَنا وَ بَيْنَ قَوْمِنا بِالْحَقِّ وَ أَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْفاتِحِينَ‌" (Our Lord, judge between us and our people with truth, and You are the best of judges).

3- It is mentioned in histories and commentaries that one day Walid ibn Yazid ibn Abd al-Malik, the tyrannical Umayyad ruler, sought an omen from the Quran to predict his future. By chance, the verse "وَ اسْتَفْتَحُوا وَ خابَ كُلُّ جَبَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ" appeared before him at the top of the page. He became extremely terrified and enraged, so much so that he tore the Quran he held in his hand! He then composed these verses:

Do you threaten every stubborn tyrant? Well, here I am, that stubborn tyrant!

When you meet your Lord on the Day of Gathering, Say: O Lord, Walid tore me to pieces!

But it was not long before he was killed by his enemies in the most gruesome manner; they cut off his head and hung it on the roof of his palace, then took it from there and hung it on the city gate. [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, p. 3579.

Ayah

مَّثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ ۖ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ كَرَمَادٍ ٱشْتَدَّتْ بِهِ ٱلرِّيحُ فِى يَوْمٍ عَاصِفٍ ۖ لَّا يَقْدِرُونَ مِمَّا كَسَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلضَّلَـٰلُ ٱلْبَعِيدُ (١٨)
A parable of those who defy their Lord: their deeds are like ashes over which the wind blows hard on a tempestuous day: they have no power over anything they have earned. That is extreme error. (18)

Commentary

Ashes Before a Gale!

In this verse, a very eloquent parable is presented regarding the deeds of disbelievers, completing the discussion of the previous verses concerning the fate of the faithless.

It states: "The parable of those who disbelieve in their Lord is that their deeds are like ashes exposed to a fierce wind on a stormy day!" (مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ أَعْمالُهُمْ كَرَمادٍ اشْتَدَّتْ بِهِ الرِّيحُ فِي يَوْمٍ عاصِفٍ).

Just as ashes are scattered by a gale—especially on a stormy day—to such an extent that no one is capable of gathering them, likewise, the deniers of truth have no power to retrieve anything from the deeds they have performed; all of it is blown away by the wind, and their hands remain empty (لا يَقْدِرُونَ مِمَّا كَسَبُوا عَلى‌ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

"And that is the far-reaching straying" (ذلِكَ هُوَ الضَّلالُ الْبَعِيدُ).

Key Points

1. Why are their deeds likened to ashes before the wind?

  1. Likening their deeds to ashes—considering that unlike soil or dust, it is not a useful substance but rather the remains of a handful of fire—shows that their deeds may have an outward appearance, but it is merely an appearance without substance. In a small pot of soil, a beautiful flower may grow, but among heaps of ashes, not even a weed will sprout!

  2. Likening the deeds of disbelievers to ashes—considering that there is no cohesion or bond between the particles of ash, and they cannot be joined together even with the help of water, as each particle quickly detaches from the other—is as if it points to the reality that, unlike believers whose deeds are integrated and interconnected, with each deed completing the other (where the spirit of Monotheism and unity exists not only among believers but also among the deeds of a single person of faith), there is no trace of this cohesion or unity of action in the work of faithless individuals.

  3. Although placing ashes before a gale causes them to scatter, He emphasizes it with the phrase فِي يَوْمٍ عاصِفٍ (on a stormy day). This is because if a gale is limited and temporary, it might lift ashes from one spot and drop them in a region not too far away. However, if the day is a stormy one where winds blow from every direction from morning until evening, it is obvious that such ashes will be so scattered that every single particle will fall in a distant location, such that they cannot be gathered by any power.

  4. If a storm blows upon a pile of straw or tree leaves and scatters them to distant places, they are still identifiable. However, particles of ash are so fine and small that once scattered, they vanish from sight as if they have been completely annihilated.

  5. Although wind, and even a gale, has constructive effects in the system of creation and nature—setting aside destructive effects which are exceptional—it is the source of the following impacts: A: It spreads the seeds of plants everywhere, acting like a gardener and farmer sowing seeds across the entire globe. B: It pollinates trees and sprinkles male pollen onto the female parts of plants. C: It moves clouds from the surface of the oceans and drives them toward dry lands. D: It gradually erodes high mountains and transforms them into soft, fertile soil. E: It transfers air from polar regions to the equator and equatorial air to cold regions, playing a decisive role in moderating the Earth's temperature. F: It agitates and waves the sea waters, turning them over and thereby aerating them; if they were to remain stagnant, they would rot. For this reason, trees, plants, and all living beings benefit from the blowing of the wind because they are worthy and capable, and each utilizes it according to its capacity.

But "ash"—this lightweight ash, this hollow and dark-faced ash, this ash in which no living creature nests, which does not turn green and does not become fertile, this ash whose particles are completely disconnected—when placed before a breeze or wind, it disintegrates and spreads so rapidly that even its useless outward appearance vanishes from sight.

2. Why are their deeds hollow?

One must see why the deeds of faithless individuals are so worthless and unstable. Why can they not benefit from the results of their actions?

The answer to this question is quite clear if we analyze it with a Monotheistic worldview and its criteria, because that which gives shape and substance to an action is the intention, motive, goal, and its plan.

If the plan, motive, and goal are healthy, valuable, and significant, the action itself will be likewise. But if we perform the greatest of deeds with a base motive, an incorrect plan, and a worthless goal, that action becomes entirely distorted and hollow, and it will be like ashes before a gale!

It is not bad to clarify this discussion with a living example: currently, programs under the title of "Human Rights" are proposed and pursued in the Western world and by great powers. The same program was pursued by the Prophets, yet the product, substance, and fruit of these two are as different as the earth and the sky.

When world-devouring powers speak of human rights, they certainly do not have a human, spiritual, or moral motive. Their goal is to create a cover for more crimes and further colonization, and the motive and plan are precisely tuned for this purpose. Therefore, if, for example, a few of their spies are chained, their cry for human rights fills the whole world. But on the day when they themselves were stained with the blood of millions in Vietnam, or when they brought about all those atrocities in our Islamic countries, human rights were consigned to oblivion; rather, human rights meant cooperation with oppressive and puppet ruling bodies!

But a true Prophet or a successor of a Prophet like Ali عليه السلام wants human rights for the real freedom of humans; he pursues it to break the shackles and chains of captivity. Whenever oppression and tyranny are inflicted upon a defenseless human, he cries out, seethes, roars, and then takes action.

Thus, the first is ashes before a gale, and the second is a blessed land for nurturing various plants, sturdy trees, flowers, and fruits.

From here, the matter that commentators have discussed also becomes clear: which deeds are meant by "deeds" in the above verse? It must be said: all deeds, even their seemingly good deeds which inwardly bore the color of polytheism and idolatry.

3. The Issue of Ihabt (Nullification of Deeds)

As we explained in Surah Al-Baqarah under verse 217, regarding the issue of "Habt" (nullification) of deeds—meaning the loss of good deeds because of bad deeds, or because of disbelief and lack of faith—there is much discussion among Islamic scholars. However, the truth is that a lack of faith, persistence and stubbornness in disbelief, and also certain actions like envy, backbiting, and murder, have such a negative impact that they blow away good deeds and virtues.

The above verse is another proof of the possibility of the nullification of deeds (for further explanation, please refer to that same discussion). [^1]

4. Do inventors and discoverers have a divine reward?

Given the above discussions, an important question arises: by studying the history of sciences, inventions, and discoveries, we see that a group of human scientists spent long years in grueling labor and endured various hardships to make an invention or discovery that would lift a burden from the shoulders of their fellow humans.

For instance, Edison, the inventor of electricity—what agonizing hardships he endured for this fruitful invention, perhaps even losing his life in this path—yet he illuminated a world, set factories in motion, and from the blessing of his invention, deep wells, green trees, and prosperous farms came into existence; in short, the face of the world was transformed.

How can it be believed that he or others like Pasteur—who, by discovering microbes, saved millions of humans from the danger of death—and dozens like him should all be sent to the depths of Hell on the grounds that they supposedly lacked faith, while individuals who in their entire lives performed no significant service for humanity should have a place in the heart of Paradise?

Answer:

From the perspective of the Islamic worldview, studying the act itself in isolation is not enough; rather, the act combined with its driver and motive has value. It is often seen that people build a hospital, a school, or another charitable structure and pretend that their goal is 100% human service to a society to which they are indebted, while beneath this cover lies another matter: preserving a position, or wealth, or attracting the attention of the masses, and consolidating their material interests, or even engaging in betrayals hidden from others!

Conversely, someone might perform a small task with complete sincerity and a 100% human and spiritual motive.

Now, the records of these great men must be examined both in terms of the act and in terms of the motive and driver, and it certainly falls into one of several cases:

A: Sometimes the primary goal of an invention is purely a destructive act (like the discovery of atomic energy, which was first carried out for the purpose of building atomic bombs); then, alongside it, benefits for mankind also emerged which were not the real goal of the inventor or discoverer, or were secondary. The fate of this group of inventors is quite clear.

B: Sometimes the inventor or discoverer's goal is material gain or fame and reputation. In reality, he is like a merchant who establishes public utility facilities for more income, creating jobs for a group and bringing products to a country, without having any goal other than obtaining income; if another job had more income, he would have gone for that.

Of course, such trade or production, if carried out according to legitimate standards, is not a wrong or forbidden act, but it is not considered an exceptionally sacred act either.

And there have been many such inventors and discoverers throughout history, and the sign of this way of thinking is that if they see that income—or more than that—can be secured through ways harmful to society (for example, making 20% profit in the pharmaceutical industry and 50% in heroin production), this specific second group prefers the latter.

The fate of this group is also clear; they have no claim either upon Allah or upon their fellow humans, and their reward was the profit and fame they sought and achieved.

C: There is a third group who certainly have human motives, or if they believe in God, divine motives. Sometimes they spend long years of their lives in the corners of laboratories in extreme misery and deprivation, hoping to serve their kind, to present a gift to the world of humanity, to unlock a chain from the foot of a suffering person, and to brush the dust off a pained face.

If such individuals have faith and a divine driver, there is no debate about them. If they do not, but their driver is human and for the people, they will undoubtedly receive a suitable reward from Allah. This reward may be in this world, or it may be in the next world. Certainly, the All-Knowing, Just God will not deprive them, but how and in what way? The details are not clear to us. We can only say: "Allah does not waste the reward of such doers of good" (Of course, if they are instances of the "ignorant who are excused" regarding the non-acceptance of faith, the matter is much clearer).

The proof for this issue, in addition to the judgment of reason, are the indications found in verses or narrations.

We have no reason that the phrase فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ [^1] should not include such persons, because "Muhsinin" (doers of good) in the Quran is not applied only to believers. Thus, we see that the brothers of Joseph, when they came to him without recognizing him and while they thought he was the Vizier of Egypt, said to him: إِنَّا نَراكَ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ "Indeed, we see you as one of the doers of good."

Furthermore, the verse فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْراً يَرَهُ وَ مَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ: "So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it," clearly includes such persons.

In a Hadith from Ali ibn Yaqtin from Imam al-Kazim عليه السلام, we read: "Among the Children of Israel, there was a man of faith who had a disbelieving neighbor. The faithless man used to treat his believing neighbor with kindness. When he died, God built a house for him that protected him from the heat of the fire... and it was said to him: 'This is because of your kind treatment of your believing neighbor.'" [^1] Also, regarding Abdullah ibn Jud'an, who was a famous polytheist of the Age of Ignorance and one of the leaders of the Quraysh, it is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "The one with the least punishment among the people of Hell is Ibn Jud'an." They asked, "O Messenger of Allah, why?" He said: "انه كان يطعم الطعام: He used to feed the hungry." [^2] In another narration, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to Adi ibn Hatim (the son of Hatim al-Tai): "Severe punishment was averted from your father because of the generosity of his soul." [^3] And in another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: that a group from Yemen came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله for "debate and dispute," and among them was a man who spoke more than everyone else and showed a particular harshness and stubbornness toward the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله became so angry that the signs were clearly visible on his blessed face. At this moment, Gabriel came and conveyed the Divine message to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as follows:

"Allah says: This is a generous man." Upon hearing this, the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله anger subsided. He turned to him and said: "My Lord has given me such a message, and were it not for that, I would have dealt with you so harshly that you would become a lesson for others."

The man asked, "Does your Lord love generosity?" He said, "Yes." He said, "I bear witness that there is no deity but 'Allah' and that you are His Messenger, and by the God who sent you, I have never turned anyone away empty-handed." [^1]

Here a question arises: it is understood from some verses and many narrations that faith, or even Wilayah (guardianship), is a condition for the acceptance of deeds or entry into Paradise. Therefore, if the best of deeds are performed by individuals lacking faith, they will not be accepted in the presence of God.

However, one can answer this question by saying that the issue of "acceptance of deeds" is one thing, and having a "suitable reward" is another. For this reason, it is famous among Islamic scholars that, for example, a prayer without presence of heart or with the commission of certain sins like backbiting is not accepted in the presence of God, even though we know such a prayer is legally valid, is an act of obedience to God's command, and is considered the fulfillment of a duty. It is certain that obedience to God's command will not be without reward.

Therefore, the acceptance of an act is its highest level, and in the case under discussion, we say the same: we say that if human and public services are accompanied by faith, they will have the highest substance; otherwise, they will not be entirely without substance or reward. Regarding entry into Paradise, we give the same answer: the reward for an act does not necessarily have to be exclusively entry into the Garden.

(This is the essence of the discussion suitable for a commentary; its details should be raised in jurisprudential discussions).
