Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ إِن يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَيَأْتِ بِخَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ (١٩)
Have you not regarded that Allah created the heavens and the earth with justice? If He wishes, He will take you away, and bring about a new creation, (19)
وَمَا ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ بِعَزِيزٍ (٢٠)
and that is not a formidable thing for Allah. (20)

Commentary

Creation is Based on Truth

Following the discussion of falsehood in the previous verse—and how it is like scattered and restless ashes that are constantly moved from one point to another by the blowing of the wind—the first verse under discussion speaks of Truth and its stability.

Addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as a model for all seekers of truth in the world, it says: "Have you not seen that Allah created the heavens and the earth in truth?"

(أَ لَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِ‌).

As Raghib states in Al-Mufradat, the word "Truth" (al-Haqq) originally means "correspondence and harmony," but its applications vary:

Sometimes "Truth" refers to an action that is created according to wisdom, calculation, and order, as we read in the Quran: هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ الشَّمْسَ ضِياءً وَ الْقَمَرَ نُوراً ... ما خَلَقَ اللَّهُ ذلِكَ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِ‌: "He is the One who made the sun a radiance and the moon a light... Allah did not create these except in truth [with calculation and wisdom]" (Yunus, 5).

Sometimes the word "Truth" is applied to the Person who performs such an action, just as this term is applied to Allah: فَذلِكمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمُ الْحَقُ‌: "That then is Allah, your True Lord" (Yunus, 32).

Sometimes "Truth" refers to a belief that corresponds to reality, such as: فَهَدَى اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لِمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ مِنَ الْحَقِ‌: "Then Allah guided those who believed to the truth of that wherein they differed" (Al-Baqarah, 213).

And sometimes it refers to a word or deed performed in the necessary measure and at its necessary time, such as: حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ مِنِّي لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ‌: "The word from Me has come true: I will surely fill Hell" (with sinners) (As-Sajdah, 13).

In any case, its opposites are "falsehood" (batil), "straying" (dalal), "play" (la'ib), futility, and the like.

As for the verse under discussion, it undoubtedly refers to the first meaning. That is, the structure of the created world—the heavens and the earth—all indicates that in their creation, there was order, calculation, wisdom, and a purpose. Allah neither needed their creation, nor did He feel loneliness from which He sought refuge, nor did He wish to remedy any deficiency in His Essence through them, for He is Self-Sufficient (Ghani) beyond all things. Rather, this vast universe is a dwelling place for the nurturing of creatures and for granting them ever-greater perfection.

He then adds that the proof He has no need for you or your faith is that: "If He wills, He can take you away and bring a new creation" (إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَ يَأْتِ بِخَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ).

A creation that would all be believers and would not commit any of your wrongdoings.

"And that is not at all difficult for Allah" (وَ ما ذلِكَ عَلَى اللَّهِ بِعَزِيزٍ).

The evidence for this statement is what we read in Surah An-Nisa: وَ إِنْ تَكْفُرُوا فَإِنَّ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ غَنِيًّا حَمِيداً ... إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ وَ يَأْتِ بِآخَرِينَ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ ذلِكَ قَدِيراً: "And if you disbelieve, then surely to Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, and Allah is Self-Sufficient, Praiseworthy... If He wills, He can take you away, O people, and bring others [in your place], and Allah is All-Powerful over that" (An-Nisa, 131-133).

This interpretation regarding the above verse has also been narrated from Ibn Abbas.

There is another possibility: that the above sentence refers to the issue of the Resurrection (Ma'ad). That is, there is no obstacle for Allah to remove all human beings and create another creation. With such power and ability, do you still have doubt and hesitation regarding the issue of the Resurrection and your return to life in another world?

Ayahs

وَبَرَزُوا۟ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا فَقَالَ ٱلضُّعَفَـٰٓؤُا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُنَّا لَكُمْ تَبَعًا فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّغْنُونَ عَنَّا مِنْ عَذَابِ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ قَالُوا۟ لَوْ هَدَىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ لَهَدَيْنَـٰكُمْ ۖ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْنَآ أَجَزِعْنَآ أَمْ صَبَرْنَا مَا لَنَا مِن مَّحِيصٍ (٢١)
Together they will be presented before Allah. Then those who were weak will say to the arrogant [leaders], ‘Indeed we were your followers. So will you avail us against Allah’s punishment in any wise?’ They will say, ‘Had Allah guided us, surely we would have guided you. It is the same to us whether we are restless or patient: there is no escape for us.’ (21)
وَقَالَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لَمَّا قُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ وَعَدَكُمْ وَعْدَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَوَعَدتُّكُمْ فَأَخْلَفْتُكُمْ ۖ وَمَا كَانَ لِىَ عَلَيْكُم مِّن سُلْطَـٰنٍ إِلَّآ أَن دَعَوْتُكُمْ فَٱسْتَجَبْتُمْ لِى ۖ فَلَا تَلُومُونِى وَلُومُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُم ۖ مَّآ أَنَا۠ بِمُصْرِخِكُمْ وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُصْرِخِىَّ ۖ إِنِّى كَفَرْتُ بِمَآ أَشْرَكْتُمُونِ مِن قَبْلُ ۗ إِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٢٢)
When the matter is all over, Satan will say, ‘Indeed Allah made you a promise that was true and I [too] made you a promise, but I failed you. I had no authority over you, except that I called you and you responded to me. So do not blame me, but blame yourselves. I cannot respond to your distress calls, neither can you respond to my distress calls. Indeed I disavow your taking me for [Allah’s] partner aforetime. There is indeed a painful punishment for the wrongdoers.’ (22)
وَأُدْخِلَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ ۖ تَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيهَا سَلَـٰمٌ (٢٣)
Those who have faith and do righteous deeds will be admitted into gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever], by the leave of their Lord. Their greeting therein will be ‘Peace!’ (23)

Commentary

The Explicit Dialogue Between Satan and His Followers

In the preceding verses, reference was made to the severe and painful punishment of the stubborn and faithless deviants; the verses under discussion pursue and complete this same theme.

First, it states: "On the Day of Resurrection, everyone—including the tyrants, the oppressors, and the disbelievers, whether they were followers or those followed, the led or the leaders—shall appear before Allah" (وَبَرَزُوا لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً) [^1]. At this moment, the weak—meaning the ignorant followers who cast themselves into the valley of perdition through blind imitation—will say to the arrogant ones who were the cause of their misguidance:

"We were your followers; is it possible now, since we have been afflicted with all this torment and calamity because of your leadership, that you might take upon yourselves a portion of our punishment so that it may be lightened for us?" (فَقَالَ الضُّعَفَاءُ لِلَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا إِنَّا كُنَّا لَكُمْ تَبَعاً فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُغْنُونَ عَنَّا مِنْ عَذَابِ اللَّهِ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

But they will immediately reply: "Had Allah guided us toward salvation from this penalty and torment, we would have guided you as well" (قَالُوا لَوْ هَدَانَا اللَّهُ لَهَدَيْنَاكُمْ).

But alas, the time for such talk has passed: "It is all the same to us whether we rage and show impatience or remain patient and enduring; there is no way of escape for us" (سَوَاءٌ عَلَيْنَا أَجَزِعْنَا أَمْ صَبَرْنَا مَا لَنَا مِنْ مَحِيصٍ).

Key Points

1- The first question that arises regarding this verse The first question that arises regarding this verse is: Are people not already manifest and apparent before the knowledge of God in this world, such that the verse says they will all appear before Allah in the Resurrection? In response to this question, many commentators have said the meaning is that human beings in this world do not feel that they and all their actions are manifest and apparent before God, but in the Resurrection, everyone will feel this presence and manifestation.

Some have also said that it refers to emerging from the graves and appearing in the court of Divine Justice for accounting.

Both of these interpretations are good, and there is no obstacle to both being included in the concept of the verse.

2- What is meant by the phrase "لَوْ هَدَانَا اللَّهُ لَهَدَيْنَاكُمْ"? Many commentators believe that the "guidance" referred to here is the path to salvation from Divine punishment in that world. This is because the "arrogant ones" say this in response to their followers who had requested them to take a share of the punishment, and the harmony between the question and answer necessitates that the meaning is guidance toward the way of deliverance from torment.

Coincidentally, this same expression (guidance) is also seen regarding reaching the blessings of Paradise, where we read from the tongues of the people of Paradise: وَقَالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي هَدَانَا لِهَذَا وَمَا كُنَّا لِنَهْتَدِيَ لَوْلَا أَنْ هَدَانَا اللَّهُ: "They say: Praise be to Allah, who guided us to such blessings, and had it not been for His grace and guidance, we would not have found the way to these" (Al-A'raf, 43).

There is also the possibility that the "leaders of misguidance," when they find themselves faced with the demands of their followers, attempt to distance the guilt from themselves—as is the custom of all standard-bearers of misguidance to blame their sabotage on others—and say with utter audacity: "What could we do? If Allah had guided us to the right path, we would have guided you too!" Meaning, we were compelled and had no will of our own.

This is the same logic of Satan, who, to exonerate himself, officially attributed compulsion to the Just God and said: "فَبِمَا أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأَقْعُدَنَّ لَهُمْ صِرَاطَكَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ": "Now that You have led me astray, I will surely sit in ambush for them on Your straight path" (and deviate them) (Al-A'raf, 16).

However, it must be noted that whether the arrogant ones like it or not, they carry the burden of responsibility for the sins of their followers according to the explicit verses of the Quran and narrations, for they were the founders of deviation and the agents of misguidance, without anything being diminished from the responsibility and punishment of the followers.

3- The most eloquent statement in condemnation of blind imitation: From the above verse, it becomes clearly evident that:

Firstly—Those who follow this person or that person with closed eyes and ears, and so to speak, hand over their reins to anyone, are weak and characterless individuals whom the Quran refers to as "الضُّعَفَاءُ" (the weak).

Secondly—The fate of both them and their leaders is the same, and these wretched souls cannot benefit from the support of these misguided leaders even in the most difficult circumstances, nor can they lighten their punishment by even an atom. Rather, perhaps with mockery, they are answered that they should not fret and wail in vain, for there is no way of deliverance or salvation!

4- "بَرَزُوا" is originally from the root "buruz," meaning to appear and come out from behind a curtain. It also means to step out of a rank and stand before an opponent in a battlefield, or what is termed "to duel."

"مَحِيصٍ" is from the root "mahs," meaning deliverance from a defect or distress.

Then, the discourse turns to another scene of the psychological punishments of the tyrants, the sinners, and the followers of devils on the Day of Resurrection, saying:

"And Satan, when the matter of accounting for the righteous and unrighteous servants has been concluded and each has reached their certain destiny, reward, or punishment, will say to his followers: 'Indeed, Allah promised you a promise of truth, and I too promised you (a hollow and worthless promise as you yourselves knew), then I failed you in my promises'" (وَقَالَ الشَّيْطَانُ لَمَّا قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ وَعَدَكُمْ وَعْدَ الْحَقِّ وَوَعَدْتُكُمْ فَأَخْلَفْتُكُمْ).

In this way, Satan joins the other arrogant ones who were leaders on the path of misguidance and aims his arrows of blame and reproach at these wretched followers.

He then adds: "I had no authority, compulsion, or obligation over you; it was only that I invited you, and you accepted of your own will and volition" (وَمَا كَانَ لِي عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطَانٍ إِلَّا أَنْ دَعَوْتُكُمْ فَاسْتَجَبْتُمْ لِي).

"Therefore, never blame me, but blame yourselves" for why you accepted my diabolical and obviously corrupt invitation (فَلَا تَلُومُونِي وَلُومُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ).

You did it to yourselves, so let the curse be upon yourselves!!

In any case, "Neither can I come to your aid against the definitive decree and punishment of the Lord, nor can you be my helpers" (مَا أَنَا بِمُصْرِخِكُمْ وَمَا أَنْتُمْ بِمُصْرِخِيَّ). "I now declare that I am weary of your shirk regarding me—that you placed obedience to me alongside obedience to Allah—and I disbelieve in it" (إِنِّي كَفَرْتُ بِمَا أَشْرَكْتُمُونِ مِنْ قَبْلُ). Now I realize that this "shirk in obedience" has made both me and you wretched—the kind of wretchedness and misery for which there is no way of reform or compensation.

Know that "for the wrongdoers, there is certainly a painful punishment" (إِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

Key Points

1- The crushing response Satan gives to his followers: Although the word "Satan" has a broad meaning that includes all rebels and tempters among Jinn and men, given the context in this verse and the preceding ones, it certainly refers here to the person of Iblis, who is considered the leader of the satans. Therefore, all commentators have chosen this interpretation [^1]. From this verse, it is clearly understood that the whisperings of Satan never take away choice and freedom of will from man; rather, he is nothing more than an inviter, and it is human beings who accept his invitation by their own will. However, it is possible that prior backgrounds and persistence in wrongdoing may bring a person to a point where a kind of state of loss of choice against temptations arises within them, just as we observe in some addicts regarding narcotics. But we know that since the cause of this state was also voluntary, whatever the result may be, it is still considered voluntary!

In verse 100 of Surah An-Nahl, we read: إِنَّمَا سُلْطَانُهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَوَلَّوْنَهُ وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِهِ مُشْرِكُونَ: "His authority is only over those who have accepted his guardianship and those who have made him a partner to Allah in the matter of obedience."

Furthermore, in this way, Satan gives a crushing response to all those who cast their sins upon him, consider him the agent of their deviations, and send curses upon him. He smashes this common, vulgar logic that a group of sinners uses to exonerate themselves. In truth, the real authority over man is his own will and his own action, and nothing else.

2- How does Satan have the ability to contact all his followers in that great gathering and subject them to his blame and taunting? This is a question raised here.

The answer is that Allah certainly grants him this ability, and this is in fact a type of psychological punishment for the followers of Satan. It is a warning to all those who tread his path in this world to see the end of their affair and their leader from now. In any case, Allah provides the means for this communication between Satan and his followers in some manner.

Interestingly, this type of encounter is not limited to Satan and his followers in the Resurrection; all the leaders of misguidance and the imams of deviation in this world have the same program. They take the hands of their followers (with their own consent, of course) and drag them into the waves of calamities and misfortunes, and when they see that things are bad, they abandon them and leave, even declaring their disassociation from them and turning to blame and reproach them, leaving them—so to speak—afflicted with loss in this world and the Hereafter.

4- "مُصْرِخ" (helper) is from the root "israkh," which originally comes from "sarakh," meaning to cry out for help. Therefore, "musrikh" means one who answers a cry for help, and "mustasrikh" means one who seeks help.

5- The meaning of making Satan a partner in the above verse is "shirk in obedience, not shirk in worship."

6- Regarding whether "إِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ" is a continuation of Satan's words or an independent sentence from the Lord, there is discussion among commentators. However, it seems more likely that it is an independent sentence from Allah, stated at the end of Satan's dialogue with his followers as an instructive lesson.

In the final verse under discussion, following the description of the state of the stubborn, faithless tyrants and their painful fate, the discourse turns to the state of the believers and their end, saying:

"And those who believed and performed righteous deeds shall be admitted into gardens of Paradise—those same gardens beneath whose trees rivers of water flow" (وَأُدْخِلَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ).

"They shall abide therein forever by the permission of their Lord" (خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ).

"And their greeting therein is 'Peace'" (تَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيهَا سَلَامٌ).

"تَحِيَّة" (greeting) is originally derived from the root "hayat" (life), then it was used as a prayer for the health and life of individuals, and it is applied to any kind of welcome, greeting, or prayer said at the beginning of a meeting.

Some commentators have said: "Tahiyyah" in the above verse is the welcome and salutation that Allah sends to the people of faith, pairing them with His blessing of safety—safety from every kind of distress and harm, and safety from every kind of war and conflict (therefore, "tahiyyatuhum" is an addition to the object, and its subject is Allah).

Others have said: What is meant here is the greeting that believers say to one another, or that the angels say to them. In any case, the word "سَلَام" (Peace/Safety), which is stated absolutely, has a concept so broad that it includes every kind of safety from every type of spiritual and physical distress and harm. [^1]

[^1]: Regarding "Salam" and "Tahiyyah," we have discussed this in detail in Volume 4, under verse 86, page 41 onwards.

[^1]: It should be noted that "بَرَزُوا" is a past tense verb, but here it carries a future meaning, because matters related to the Resurrection are so certain and infallible that they are expressed in many verses of the Quran with the past tense, just as we might say of someone who is ill and we are certain will die: "So-and-so has passed away!"

[^1]: For more explanation regarding the meaning of "Satan" in the Quran, refer to Volume 1, page 136 onwards, under the verses related to the creation of Adam.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ ضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا كَلِمَةً طَيِّبَةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ أَصْلُهَا ثَابِتٌ وَفَرْعُهَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ (٢٤)
Have you not regarded how Allah has drawn a parable? A good word is like a good tree: its roots are steady and its branches are in the sky. (24)
تُؤْتِىٓ أُكُلَهَا كُلَّ حِينٍۭ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهَا ۗ وَيَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٢٥)
It gives its fruit every season by the leave of its Lord. Allah draws these parables for mankind so that they may take admonition. (25)
وَمَثَلُ كَلِمَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ ٱجْتُثَّتْ مِن فَوْقِ ٱلْأَرْضِ مَا لَهَا مِن قَرَارٍ (٢٦)
And the parable of a bad word is that of a bad tree: uprooted from the ground, it has no stability. (26)
يُثَبِّتُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱلْقَوْلِ ٱلثَّابِتِ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَيُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ ۚ وَيَفْعَلُ ٱللَّهُ مَا يَشَآءُ (٢٧)
Allah fortifies those who have faith with a constant creed in the life of this world and in the Hereafter, and Allah leads astray the wrongdoers, and Allah does whatever He wishes. (27)

Commentary

The Pure Tree and the Evil Tree

Here, another scene of the embodiment of Truth and Falsehood, Disbelief and Faith, and the Pure and the Evil is presented through a striking, profound, and meaningful parable, completing the discussions of the previous verses on this subject.

First, He says: "Have you not considered how Allah sets forth a parable of a pure word, likening it to a pure tree?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ ضَرَبَ اللَّهُ مَثَلًا كَلِمَةً طَيِّبَةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ).

Then, He proceeds to describe the characteristics of this Shajarah Tayyibah (pure and blessed tree), touching upon all its dimensions in brief phrases.

However, before we examine the characteristics of this pure tree alongside the Quran, we must see what is meant by the "Pure Word" (Kalimah Tayyibah).

Some commentators have interpreted it as the word of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the phrase La ilaha illa Allah (There is no god but Allah).

While others consider it a reference to Divine commands and decrees.

Others identify it as Faith, which is the essence and concept of La ilaha illa Allah.

Some have interpreted it as the "Believer" (Mu'min) himself.

And some have suggested constructive methods and programs in its interpretation [^1]. However, considering the breadth of the concept and content of the "Pure Word," it can be said to encompass all of these. This is because "Word" (Kalimah) in its broad sense includes all beings; for this reason, creatures are referred to as the "Word of Allah" (Kalimat Allah) [^2].

[^1]: Refer to the commentaries of Majma' al-Bayan, Qurtubi, Fi Zilal, and Fakhr Razi.

[^2]: We have discussed the concept of "Word" in the commentary of Surah Al-An'am, Verse 115, in Volume 5 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 411.

And "Pure" (Tayyib) refers to anything clean and wholesome. Consequently, this parable includes every tradition, instruction, program, method, every action, every human being, and in short, every pure and blessed entity. All of these are like a pure tree with the following characteristics:

  1. It is a living entity capable of growth and development, not soulless, nor solid and motionless, but rather dynamic, growing, and constructive for both itself and others (the use of the term "Tree" expresses this reality).

  2. This tree is pure (Tayyib). But in what sense? Since no specific part was singled out, it implies that it is pure in every aspect: its appearance is pure, its fruit is pure, its blossoms and flowers are pure, its shade is pure, and even the breeze that rises from it is pure.

  3. This tree possesses a calculated system; it has a root and branches, each with a mission and duty. Fundamentally, the existence of an "origin" (Asl) and "branches" (Far') within it is evidence of a calculated system governing it.

  4. "Its root is firm and established" (أَصْلُها ثابِتٌ), such that storms and gales cannot uproot it. It has the strength to suspend and maintain its sky-reaching branches in the air, under the sunlight and in the open atmosphere; for the higher a branch reaches, the stronger the root it must rely upon.

  5. The branches of this pure tree are not in a lowly or limited environment; rather, the high heaven is its place. These branches have pierced the heart of the air and extended into it; yes, "its branches are in the sky" (وَ فَرْعُها فِي السَّماءِ).

Clearly, the higher the branches are raised, the further they are from the dust and pollution of the earth, and the purer their fruits will be. They benefit more from sunlight and fresh air and transmit these better to their pure fruits [^1].

  1. This pure tree is a fruitful tree, not like trees that bear no fruit. Therefore, it is productive and "yields its fruit" (تُؤْتِي أُكُلَها).

  2. But not just in one or two seasons; rather, in every season. That is, "at all times" (كُلَّ حِينٍ), whenever you reach out toward its branches, you do not return empty-handed.

  3. Its fruiting is not haphazard either; rather, it is subject to the laws of creation and, according to a Divine tradition, it bestows this fruit upon everyone "by the permission of its Lord" (بِإِذْنِ رَبِّها).

Now let us reflect deeply: where do we find these characteristics and blessings? Certainly, in the word of Monotheism and its content, in a monotheistic and knowledgeable person, and in a constructive and pure program. These are all growing, striving, and moving. They all have firm and stable roots; they all have numerous branches reaching toward the sky, far from physical pollutions and impurities. They are all fruitful, radiating light, and bestowing grace.

Whoever comes to their side and reaches out to the branches of their existence, at whatever time it may be, benefits from their delicious, fragrant, and invigorating fruits. The gales of events and harsh storms and problems do not move them from their place. The horizon of their thought is not limited to this small world; they tear through the veils of time and space and advance toward eternity and infinity.

Their programs are not born of whim or desire, but are all by the permission of the Lord and according to His command. This movement, dynamism, and fruitfulness also stem from this very source.

Great men of faith—these pure words of the Lord—their lives are a source of blessing, and their deaths are a cause for movement. Their works, their words and speeches, their students and books, their glorious history, and even their silent graves are all inspiring, constructive, and educational.

Yes, "Allah sets forth parables for mankind so that they may take heed" (وَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْأَمْثالَ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ).

Here, a question has been raised among commentators: does a tree with the above attributes exist externally such that the "Pure Word" is likened to it (a tree that is green and full of fruit in all seasons of the year)? Some believe it exists and that it is the date palm, and for this reason, they have been forced to interpret "at all times" (Kulla hinin) as "six months."

However, there is no necessity at all to insist on the external existence of such a tree. Rather, we have many metaphors in various languages that have no external existence. For example, we say the Quran is like a sun that never sets (while we know the sun always sets), or that my separation is like a night that has no end (while we know every night ends).

In any case, since the goal of a parable is to visualize truths and place intellectual matters into a sensory framework, such metaphors face no obstacle; rather, they are completely pleasant, effective, and attractive.

At the same time, there are trees in this very world whose branches are never devoid of fruit throughout the year. We ourselves have seen some trees in tropical regions that simultaneously had fruit and had blossomed again, with the precursors of new fruit being prepared, even though it was the winter season!

Since one of the best ways to explain matters is to use the method of contrast and comparison, He immediately describes the opposite of the "Pure Tree" in the next verse: But "the parable of an evil word is like an evil tree, uprooted from the surface of the earth, having no stability" (وَ مَثَلُ كَلِمَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ اجْتُثَّتْ مِنْ فَوْقِ الْأَرْضِ ما لَها مِنْ قَرارٍ).

The "Evil Word" (Kalimah Khabithah) is the word of disbelief and polytheism, foul and ominous speech, misleading and false programs, impure and contaminated humans, and in short, anything evil and impure.

Naturally, an ugly and ominous tree whose root has been severed has neither growth nor development, neither progress nor perfection, neither flowers nor fruit, neither shade nor beauty, nor stability or permanence. It is a piece of wood fit for nothing but burning and fire; rather, it is an obstacle in the path, a nuisance to travelers, and sometimes stinging, wounding, and harmful to people!

Interestingly, in describing the "Pure Tree," the Quran speaks in detail, but when describing the "Evil Tree," it passes over it with a single short sentence, saying only: "uprooted from the surface of the earth, having no stability." Once it is established that this tree is rootless, the fate of its branches, leaves, flowers, and fruit is already clear.

Furthermore, this is a type of eloquence where one elaborates on all the characteristics when mentioning the "Beloved," but when mentioning the "Hated," one passes over it with a single crushing sentence! Again, we see commentators discussing which tree this "object of comparison" (Mushabbah bih) is. Some have considered it the "Colocynth" (Hanzal) tree, which has very bitter and bad fruit, and some have called it the "Dodder" (Kashut), a type of tangled plant that winds around thorn bushes in deserts and climbs them, having neither root nor leaf (note that Shajar in the Arabic language refers to both trees and plants).

But as we explained in the commentary of the "Pure Tree," there is no necessity for the "object of comparison" in every parable to exist externally with all those attributes. Rather, the goal is to visualize the true face of the word of polytheism, deviant programs, and evil people—that they are like trees whose everything is evil and impure, having no fruit, result, or benefit except nuisance and causing trouble.

Moreover, the existence of an impure tree that has been uprooted and placed in the desert against the heart of a storm and gale is not uncommon.

Since in the previous verses, through two vivid parables, the state of "Faith" and "Disbelief," the "Believer" and the "Disbeliever," and generally "every pure thing" and "impure thing" was visualized, in the last verse under discussion, He addresses the outcome of their work and their final destiny. First, He says: "Allah keeps firm those who believe, with the firm word and belief, both in this world and in the Hereafter" (يُثَبِّتُ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ).

This is because their faith was not a superficial and shaky faith, and their personality was not a false and fickle one; rather, it was a "Pure Tree" whose root was firm and whose branches were in the sky. Since no one is independent of Allah's grace—or in other words, every blessing ultimately returns to His Pure Essence—these truthful, firm-footed believers, relying on Allah's grace, stand firm like a mountain against every event.

The pitfalls that are inevitable in life appear in their path, but Allah preserves them. "Satans" from every side whisper to them and, using the various glitters of this world, strive to make them slip, but their God keeps them. Hellish powers and stone-hearted tyrants strive with various threats to make them surrender, but Allah keeps them firm because they have chosen a firm and solid root for themselves.

Interestingly, this Divine preservation and stability encompass their entire lives—both the life of this world and the life of that world. Here, they remain firm in faith and purity, and their skirts will be free from the stain of impurities; and there, they will remain eternal in the endless blessings of Allah.

Then, He turns to their opposite and says: "And Allah leaves the wrongdoers to stray, and Allah does whatever He wills" (وَ يُضِلُّ اللَّهُ الظَّالِمِينَ وَ يَفْعَلُ اللَّهُ ما يَشاءُ).

We have said many times that wherever there is mention of guidance and misguidance attributed to Allah, the first steps are taken by the human himself. Allah's work is the very effect He has created in every action, and also Allah's work is the bestowal of favors and blessings or the withdrawal of blessings, which He decrees according to the merit or lack of merit of individuals (reflect carefully).

The use of the term "Wrongdoers" (Zalimin) after the phrase "Allah leaves to stray" is the best evidence for this subject. That is, until someone becomes contaminated with injustice and oppression, the blessing of guidance will not be withdrawn from him. But after contamination with injustice and tyranny, the darkness of sin envelops his being, and the light of Divine guidance leaves his heart. This is the very essence of choice and free will. Of course, if he quickly changes his path, the way of salvation is open; but after sin becomes established, returning will be very difficult.

Key Points

1. Does "Hereafter" in the last verse refer to the Grave?

In numerous narrations, we read that Allah keeps the human firm on the path of faith when entering his grave, in the face of the questions the angels ask about his identity; and this is the meaning of "Allah keeps firm those who believe, with the firm word... in this world and in the Hereafter."

In some of these narrations, the word "Grave" (Qabr) is explicitly mentioned [^1]. Meanwhile, in other narrations, we read that Satan comes to the believers at the time of death and whispers from the left and right to mislead them, but Allah does not allow him to mislead the believer; and this is the meaning of "Allah keeps firm those who believe..."

Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said: "Indeed, Satan comes to a man from our followers at his death, from his right and from his left, to mislead him from what he is upon, but Allah عز و جل refuses that for him; [this is] the word of Allah عز و جل: 'Allah keeps firm those who believe with the firm word in the life of this world and in the Hereafter'" [^1].

And most commentators—according to the report of the great commentator Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan—have accepted this interpretation.

Perhaps this is because the abode of the Hereafter is neither a place for slipping nor a place for action, but only a place for facing results. However, at the moment of death and even in the world of Barzakh (the world between this world and the Hereafter), the possibility of slipping exists more or less, and it is here that Allah's grace rushes to the aid of the human, preserving him and keeping him firm.

2. The Role of Stability and Steadfastness

Among all the attributes mentioned in the above verses for the "Pure Tree" and the "Evil Tree," the issue of stability and lack thereof stands out most of all. Even in describing the fruit of this tree in the last verse under discussion, we read: Allah keeps the believers firm because of their firm belief in this world and the Hereafter. In this way, the extraordinary importance of the issue of stability and its role is highlighted.

Much has been said regarding the factors of success for great men, but among all of them, the issue of steadfastness and perseverance must be named in the first rank. There are many who possess average intelligence and talent, or whose initiative is completely average, yet they have achieved great successes in life, which upon investigation, we see have no reason other than stability and steadfastness!

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, pp. 540-541.

From a social perspective, the progress of any effective program is possible only under the shadow of stability; for this reason, all the efforts of destroyers are employed to eliminate stability. Fundamentally, true believers must be recognized by their stability and steadfastness in the face of harsh events and the storms of life.

3. The Pure and Evil Trees in Islamic Narrations

As we said above, the words "Pure" (Tayyibah) and "Evil" (Khabithah) used to describe these two trees have a broad concept that includes every type of person, program, school of thought, idea, speech, and action. However, in some Islamic narrations, they have been interpreted as specific instances, which clearly do not intend to be exclusive.

Among them, in the book al-Kafi, from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of the phrase "like a pure tree, its root is firm and its branches are in the sky," it is narrated:

"The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله is its root, and the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام is its branch, and the Imams from their progeny are its limbs, and the knowledge of the Imams is its fruit, and their believing followers are its leaves. Is there anything else?" The narrator says: I said, "No, by Allah!" He said: "By Allah, when a believer is born, a leaf sprouts on it, and when a true believer dies, a leaf falls from it!" [^1]

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 535.

In another narration, the same meaning is quoted from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, and at its end, we read that the narrator asked: what is the meaning of the phrase "it yields its fruit at all times by the permission of its Lord"? The Imam said: "It refers to the knowledge of the Imams that reaches you every year from every region" [^1]. In other narrations, we read that the "Pure Tree" is the Prophet, Ali, Fatimah, and their children, and the "Evil Tree" is the Umayyads [^2]. In some narrations, it is also reported that the Pure Tree is the date palm and the Evil Tree is the colocynth bush [^3]. In any case, there is no contradiction among these interpretations, and there is harmony between them and what we mentioned above regarding the generality of the verse's meaning, as these are its manifestations (masadiq).

[^1] and [^2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, pp. 538 and 535.

[^3]: Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur, as quoted in al-Mizan under the verse discussed.

Ayahs

۞ أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ كُفْرًا وَأَحَلُّوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ دَارَ ٱلْبَوَارِ (٢٨)
Have you not regarded those who have changed Allah’s blessing with ingratitude, and landed their people in the house of ruin? (28)
جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَوْنَهَا ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْقَرَارُ (٢٩)
—hell, which they shall enter, and it is an evil abode! (29)
وَجَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ أَندَادًا لِّيُضِلُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ ۗ قُلْ تَمَتَّعُوا۟ فَإِنَّ مَصِيرَكُمْ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ (٣٠)
They have set up equals to Allah, to lead [people] astray from His way. Say, ‘Enjoy [for a while], for indeed your destination is hellfire!’ (30)

Commentary

The Consequence of Ingratitude for Blessings

In these verses, the address is directed toward the Prophet, and in reality, it presents a depiction of one of the instances of the "evil tree" (shajarah khabithah).

First, He says: "Have you not seen those who exchanged the favor of Allah for ingratitude" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ كُفْراً).

"And settled their people in the abode of ruin—the land of destruction and annihilation" (وَ أَحَلُّوا قَوْمَهُمْ دارَ الْبَوارِ).

These are the very roots of the evil tree and the leaders of disbelief and deviation. Blessings such as the existence of the Prophet—than which there is no greater blessing—were placed in their laps. By utilizing this blessing on the path of felicity, they could have traveled a hundred-year journey in a single night. However, blind prejudice, stubbornness, egoism, and self-worship caused them to cast aside this greatest of blessings. Not only did they do this themselves, but they also tempted their people into this act, bringing them destruction and misery as a gift.

Although great commentators, following narrations found in Islamic sources, have sometimes interpreted this "blessing" as the existence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and sometimes as the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, and have identified those who were ungrateful for this blessing as the "Banu Umayyah" and "Banu Mughirah," or sometimes all the disbelievers of the Prophet's era, the concept of the verse is certainly broad. It is not restricted to a specific group and includes all those who are ungrateful for any of Allah's blessings.

Furthermore, the above verse establishes the reality that the benefit of utilizing divine blessings—especially the blessing of the leadership of great guides, which is among the most important blessings—returns to man himself. Conversely, ingratitude for these blessings and turning one's back on this leadership has no end other than destruction and being toppled into the "Abode of Ruin" (Dar al-Bawar).

The Quran then interprets "Dar al-Bawar" as follows: "[It is] Hell, which they will [enter to] burn therein, and wretched is the settlement" (جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَوْنَها وَ بِئْسَ الْقَرارُ) [1].

In the next verse, it points to one of the worst types of ingratitude for blessings that they committed, saying: "And they set up equals to Allah to mislead [others] from His way" (وَ جَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ أَنْداداً لِيُضِلُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ).

For a few days, under the shadow of this polytheism, disbelief, and the diversion of people's thoughts from the religion and the path of Truth, they enjoy the benefits of material life, leadership, and rule over the people.

O Prophet! "Say, 'Enjoy yourselves, for indeed, your destination is the Fire'" (قُلْ تَمَتَّعُوا فَإِنَّ مَصِيرَكُمْ إِلَى النَّارِ).

Even though this life of yours is not truly a life but rather misery, and this leadership and rule of yours have no value as they are nothing but criminality, trouble, and calamity, nevertheless, this life is considered "enjoyment" (tamattu') in comparison to the end that awaits you. This is similar to what we read in another verse: "قُلْ تَمَتَّعْ بِكُفْرِكَ قَلِيلًا إِنَّكَ مِنْ أَصْحابِ النَّارِ" ("Say, 'Enjoy your disbelief for a little; indeed, you are of the companions of the Fire'") (Az-Zumar, 8).

Key Points

1- In common expressions, it is said that a certain person was ungrateful for Allah's blessing, but in the above verse we read: "they exchanged the favor of Allah for disbelief and ingratitude." This specific expression may be due to one of two reasons:

A) The intention is the exchange of "gratitude" for the blessing with "ingratitude." That is, it was necessary for them to be grateful for the blessings of the Lord, but they exchanged this gratitude for disbelief. (In reality, the word "gratitude" is implied, as if it were: those who exchanged the gratitude for Allah's blessing for disbelief).

B) The intention is that they exchanged the "blessing" itself for "disbelief." In reality, divine blessings are tools whose method of utilization depends on the will of man himself. Just as it is possible to utilize blessings on the path of faith, happiness, and goodness, they can also be employed on the path of disbelief, oppression, and evil. These blessings are like raw materials with which all kinds of products can be made, but they were originally created for goodness and felicity.

[Ingratitude for blessings is any form of deviant utilization.]

"Ingratitude for blessings" (Kufran al-Ni'mah) is not merely for a person to speak ungratefully to Allah; rather, any form of deviant utilization or misuse of a blessing is ingratitude.

Fundamentally, the reality of ingratitude is this, while verbal ungratefulness is of the second degree.

Just as gratitude for a blessing—as we have said previously—means spending the blessing toward the purpose for which it was created, and verbal thanks is of the second degree; if you say "Alhamdulillah" a thousand times with your tongue but practically misuse the blessing, you have committed ingratitude!

In this very era in which we live, the most prominent example of this "exchanging blessing for ingratitude" can be seen. The various forces of the natural world have been harnessed by man in the light of God-given intelligence and human initiative, and have been put to work in the path of his interests.

Scientific discoveries and industrial inventions have transformed the face of this world; heavy burdens have been lifted from the shoulders of humans and placed upon the wheels of factories.

Divine favors and blessings are more abundant than at any other time, and the means for publishing thoughts, expanding science and knowledge, and awareness of all the world's news have become accessible to everyone. In such an era, the people of this world should have been happy humans from every perspective, both materially and spiritually.

However, because of exchanging these great divine blessings for disbelief and spending the magnificent forces of nature in the way of rebellion and injustice, and employing inventions and discoveries in the path of destructive goals—such that every new industrial phenomenon is first utilized for destruction and its positive aspects come second—in short, this great ingratitude, which is the result of distancing oneself from the constructive teachings of the Prophets of Allah, has caused them to lead their people and population to the "Abode of Ruin."

That very Abode of Ruin which is a collection of regional and global wars with all their destructive effects, as well as insecurities, oppressions, corruptions, colonialisms, and exploitations that eventually seize the founders themselves, as we have seen in the past and see with our own eyes today.

How interestingly the Quran predicted that any people or nation that is ungrateful for Allah's blessings, their path is toward the Abode of Ruin.

3- "Andad" (أنداد) is the plural of "Nidd" (ند), meaning "like" or "equal." However, as Raghib in Al-Mufradat and Zabidi in Taj al-Arus (quoting some linguists) have mentioned, "Nidd" is said of something that has an essential (substantial) similarity to something else, whereas "Mithl" (مثل) is applied to any kind of similarity. Therefore, "Nidd" has a deeper and more expressive meaning than "Mithl."

According to this meaning, we infer from the above verse that the effort of the leaders of disbelief was to carve out partners for Allah and present them as similar to Allah in the essence of His being, so as to turn the attention of Allah's creation away from His worship and achieve their own sinister goals.

Sometimes they would assign a portion of sacrifices to them, sometimes they considered a part of divine blessings (such as certain livestock) as belonging specifically to idols, and at times, through worship, they considered them on par with Allah.

Most impudently of all, in their Hajj ceremonies during the Age of Ignorance—where they had mixed the religion of Abraham with a multitude of superstitions—when saying the "Talbiyah," they would say:

"Labbayk, You have no partner—except a partner who is Yours!—You own him and whatever he owns!" [1]

[^1]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, under the verse being discussed.

Ayahs

قُل لِّعِبَادِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يُقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ سِرًّا وَعَلَانِيَةً مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِىَ يَوْمٌ لَّا بَيْعٌ فِيهِ وَلَا خِلَـٰلٌ (٣١)
Tell My servants who have faith to maintain the prayer and to spend out of what We have provided them with, secretly and openly, before there comes a day on which there will be neither any bargaining nor friendship. (31)
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَأَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَخْرَجَ بِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ رِزْقًا لَّكُمْ ۖ وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلْفُلْكَ لِتَجْرِىَ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِۦ ۖ وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرَ (٣٢)
It is Allah who created the heavens and the earth, and He sends down water from the sky and with it He brings forth crops for your sustenance. And He disposed the ships for you[r benefit] so that they may sail at sea by His command, and He disposed the rivers for you. (32)
وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ دَآئِبَيْنِ ۖ وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ (٣٣)
He disposed the sun and the moon for you, constant [in their courses], and He disposed the night and the day, (33)
وَءَاتَىٰكُم مِّن كُلِّ مَا سَأَلْتُمُوهُ ۚ وَإِن تَعُدُّوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ لَا تُحْصُوهَآ ۗ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ (٣٤)
and He gave you all that you had asked Him. If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them. Indeed man is most unfair and ungrateful! (34)

Commentary

The Greatness of Man from the Perspective of the Quran

Following the previous verses which spoke of the agenda of the polytheists and those who were ungrateful for Divine blessings—ultimately leading themselves to the Abode of Ruin—the verses under discussion speak of the agenda of the true servants and His infinite blessings descended upon the people.

First, It says: "Tell My servants who have believed to establish prayer and spend from what We have provided them, secretly and openly" (قُلْ لِعِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يُقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَ يُنْفِقُوا مِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ سِرًّا وَ عَلانِيَةً).

"Before a Day comes in which there is no exchange," such that one could purchase salvation and escape from punishment through trade, "nor any friendship" that would be of avail (مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ يَوْمٌ لا بَيْعٌ فِيهِ وَ لا خِلالٌ).

Then, He proceeds to introduce Allah through His blessings—the kind of introduction that awakens His love in hearts and compels man to bow before His greatness and grace. This is because it is a matter of human nature (fitrah) that man feels affection and love toward someone who has helped him and whose grace and mercy encompass him. He expresses this subject through several verses as follows: "Allah is He who created the heavens and the earth" (اللَّهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ).

"And sent down water from the sky, thereby bringing forth various fruits as provision for you" (وَ أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَخْرَجَ بِهِ مِنَ الثَّمَراتِ رِزْقاً لَكُمْ).

"He subjected the ships to you," both in terms of their building materials created in nature and their motive power, which are the steady winds over the surface of the oceans (وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْفُلْكَ).

"So that these ships may sail upon the oceans by His command," cleaving through the waters toward their intended shores, easily transporting humans and their required goods from one point to another (لِتَجْرِيَ فِي الْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِ).

Likewise, "He subjected the rivers to you" (وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْأَنْهارَ).

So that you may irrigate your crops with their life-giving water, quench your thirst and that of your livestock, and often utilize their surface as a smooth path for travel by ships and boats, as well as benefit from their fish and even the pearls found in their depths.

Not only did He subject the earthly creatures to you, but "He subjected to you the sun and the moon, both constant in their courses" (وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الشَّمْسَ وَ الْقَمَرَ دائِبَيْنِ) [1]. Not just the entities of this world, but even their transient states were brought under your command, just as "He subjected to you the night and the day" (وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ وَ النَّهارَ).

[1]: "Dā’ibayn" is derived from the root "da’ūb," meaning to continue a task according to a fixed habit or tradition. Since the sun and the moon have continued their program of providing light, nurturing living beings, creating tides in the oceans, and other services for millions of years in a fixed and specific manner, no better term than "dā’ibayn" can be imagined for them.

"And He gave you of all that you asked Him," providing whatever you needed for body and soul, individual and society, and for prosperity and happiness (وَ آتاكُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ ما سَأَلْتُمُوهُ). And thus, "if you should count the blessings of Allah, you could never enumerate them" (وَ إِنْ تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ لا تُحْصُوها).

For the material and spiritual blessings of the Lord have so thoroughly encompassed your entire existence and living environment that they are beyond counting. Furthermore, what you know of Divine blessings compared to what you do not know is like a drop compared to the sea.

Yet, despite all this Divine grace and mercy, man remains unjust and ungrateful (إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ).

Blessings which, if used correctly, could have turned the entire world into a garden and implemented the blueprint of a "Utopia," have—due to misuse, oppression, and ingratitude—reached a point where the horizon of life is darkened, the nectar of life has become a soul-crushing poison, and a mass of agonizing problems has placed shackles and chains upon his hands and feet.

Key Points

1- A Link with the Creator and a Link with the Creation

Once again in these verses, in organizing the program for true believers, we encounter the issue of "Salat" (prayer) and "Infaq" (spending). At first glance, this might raise the question: why, among all the practical programs of Islam, are only these two points highlighted?

The reason is that Islam has various dimensions that can be summarized into three parts: man's relationship with God, man's relationship with God's creation, and man's relationship with himself. The third part is actually a result of the first and second. The two aforementioned programs (prayer and spending) are each a symbol of one of the first two dimensions.

Prayer is a manifestation of every kind of relationship with God, as this relationship is better defined in prayer than in any other act. Spending from what God has provided—considering its broad meaning which includes every material and spiritual blessing—is a symbol of the link with the creation.

Of course, considering that the Surah we are discussing is "Meccan," and at the time of its revelation the law of Zakat had not yet been revealed, this "spending" cannot be restricted to Zakat; rather, it has a broad meaning that includes Zakat even after its revelation.

In any case, faith is deep-rooted only if it manifests in action, drawing man closer to God on one hand and to His servants on the other!

2- Why Secretly and Openly?

Repeatedly in the verses of the Quran, we read that the spending or charities of true believers are "in secret and in public." Thus, in addition to stating spending in its broad sense, attention is also given to its quality. This is because sometimes secret spending is more effective and preserves dignity, while at other times, if it is done openly, it serves to encourage others, acts as a model for demonstrating Islamic programs, and is considered a glorification of religious symbols. Furthermore, cases arise where the recipient might feel embarrassed by receiving charity.

Currently, while we are at war with a bloodthirsty enemy (or any Muslim nation facing such a situation), people of faith head toward the borders and war zones every day with large quantities of various life necessities to help war victims, the wounded, the disabled, or the warriors themselves. This news is reflected in all mass media to serve as proof of the unreserved sympathy and support of the entire Muslim nation for its warriors, as a sign of the living human spirit among the general public, and as an encouragement for those who have fallen behind this caravan to join it as soon as possible. Obviously, in such cases, public spending is more effective.

Some commentators have also said regarding the difference between the two: public spending relates to obligatory duties (Wajibat), where there is usually no aspect of showing off, as performing one's duty is required of everyone and cannot be a hidden matter. However, recommended spending (Mustahabb), being something beyond the obligatory duty, might be accompanied by ostentation and hypocrisy; therefore, its being hidden is better.

It seems this interpretation is not universal but is actually a branch of the first interpretation.

3- On That Day There is No "Bay'" or "Khilal"

We know that the nature of the Day of Resurrection is the receiving of results and reaching the reactions and reflections of deeds. Thus, no one there can give a ransom to be saved from punishment. Even if, hypothetically, all the wealth and riches on earth were in his possession and he spent it to reduce a fraction of the punishment for his deeds, it would not be possible. This is because the "Abode of Action," which is the life of this world, has had its record folded, and that place is the "Abode of Reckoning."

Likewise, the bond of material friendship with anyone in any form cannot be a means of deliverance there (note that khilal and khullah mean friendship).

In simple terms, in the life of this world, to escape the clutches of punishment, people often resort to money or "connections"—that is, they act through "bribes" and "relationships" to neutralize punishments.

If they imagine that such programs are possible there as well, it is proof of their ignorance and ultimate folly.

From this, it becomes clear that the denial of the existence of khullah (friendship) in this verse does not contradict the friendship of believers with one another in the Hereafter, which is explicitly mentioned in some verses, because that is a spiritual friendship and affection under the shadow of faith.

As for the issue of "Intercession" (Shafa'at), as we have said many times, there is no material concept in it whatsoever. Rather, according to the explicit verses revealed in this regard, it exists only under the shadow of spiritual links and a type of merit acquired through certain good deeds, the explanation of which we provided under verse 254 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

4- All Beings Bow to Your Command, O Man!

Once again in these verses, we encounter the subjection of various entities of the earth and sky to man, with emphasis placed on six parts: The subjection of ships, the subjection of rivers, the subjection of the sun, the subjection of the moon, the subjection of the night, and the subjection of the day—which comprise part of the sky, part of the earth, and part of the phenomena between the two (night and day).

We have said before, and it is necessary to remind again, that from the perspective of the Quran, man possesses such greatness that all these beings have been subjected to him by the command of "Allah." This means either the reins of their control are in man's hands, or they move in service of man's interests. In any case, such greatness has been given to this human that he has become a supreme goal in the collection of creation.

The sun shines for him, warms his bed, grows various plants for him, cleanses his living environment from the filth of intrusive microbes, creates joy and happiness, and shows him the path of life.

The moon is the lamp for his dark nights; it is a natural and eternal calendar. The tides caused by the moon solve many of man's problems, irrigate many trees (due to the rising water of rivers near the seas), set the silent and stagnant sea into motion, protecting it from stagnation and rot, and provide necessary oxygen to the living creatures of the seas through its waves.

Winds move the ships in the heart of the oceans, and these very ships and seas constitute man's greatest vessels and widest roads, to the point that sometimes ships as large as a city, with the same population that lives in a small town, move across the expanse of the oceans.

Rivers are at his service, irrigating his crops, quenching the thirst of his livestock, refreshing his living environment, and even nurturing his food (fish) within themselves for him.

The darkness of the night covers him like a garment, granting him peace and comfort, and like a fan, it mitigates the scorching heat of the sun, granting him new soul and life.

And finally, the light of the day invites him to movement and effort, creates warmth and heat, and generates activity and motion everywhere.

In short, all are constant and obedient for his sake. Describing all these blessings, in addition to creating a new personality in man and making him aware of the greatness of his own status, also awakens his sense of gratitude.

From this explanation, it is also concluded that "subjection" (taskhir) in the culture of the Quran comes with two meanings: one is being in the service of man's interests and benefits (like the subjection of the sun and moon), and the other is having the reins of control in the hands of mankind (like the subjection of ships and seas).

The idea held by some that these verses refer to the modern technical term for "conquest" (like the conquest of the moon by space travelers) does not seem correct. This is because we read in some verses of the Quran: "And He has subjected to you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, all from Him" (Jathiyah-13), which shows that everything in the heavens and everything on the earth is subjected to man, while we know that space travelers' journeying to all celestial bodies is certainly impossible.

Yes, we have other verses in the Quran that might refer to this type of conquest, which, God willing, we will discuss in the commentary of Surah "Ar-Rahman" (we also had a discussion regarding the subjection of beings to man under verse 2 of Surah Ar-Ra'd).

5- Dā’ibayn

We said "dā’ib" is from the root "da’ūb," meaning the continuity of something in performing a program as a state and tradition. Of course, the sun does not move around the earth; it is the earth that revolves around the sun, and we perceive the sun as circling us. However, the meaning of "dā’ib" does not imply movement in space; rather, the concept of continuity in performing a task and a program is embedded in it. We know that the sun and the moon perform their program of illumination and nurturing for the planet Earth and humans continuously and with a perfectly organized calculation (and it should not be forgotten that one of the meanings of da’b is habit) [1].

6- Does He Give Us Everything We Ask of Him?

In the above verses, we read that Allah graced you and gave you a portion of what you asked of Him (note that "min" in the phrase "مِنْ كُلِّ ما سَأَلْتُمُوهُ" is partitive).

This is because it often happens that man asks God for something that certainly contains harm, loss, or even his destruction, and he does not know it. But Allah, the All-Knowing, All-Wise, and All-Merciful, never responds to such a request.

In exchange, perhaps many times man does not ask for something with his tongue, but he desires it with the language of his state (lisan al-hal), his nature, his disposition, and his very existence, and God gives it to him. There is no obstacle to the "asking" in the phrase "ما سَأَلْتُمُوهُ" including both the asking of the tongue and the asking of the state.

7- Why His Blessings Cannot Be Counted

It is a reality that our entire existence is drowned in His blessings. If we examine various books of natural sciences, anthropology, psychology, botany, and the like, we will see how vast the scope of these blessings is. Fundamentally, as that great man of letters said, in every breath two blessings are present, and for every blessing, a thanks is due.

Beyond this, we know that in a human body, there are on average ten million billion living cells, each forming an active unit of our body. This number is so large that if we wanted to count these cells, it would take hundreds of years!

And this is just one part of God's blessings toward us. Therefore, truly, if we wish to count His blessings, we are unable: "And if you should count the blessings of Allah, you could never enumerate them."

Within human blood, there are two groups of corpuscles (small living entities floating in the blood that carry heavy vital duties): millions of red blood cells whose duty is to deliver atmospheric oxygen for the metabolism of body cells, and millions of white blood cells whose duty is to guard human health against the invasion of microbes. The wonder is that they are constantly at the service of man without rest or sleep.

In this state, can we ever enumerate His endless blessings?

8- Alas, Man is "Unjust" and "Ungrateful"

From the previous discussions, we reached the reality that Allah, with all these beings He has subjected to man's command and all those blessings He has bestowed upon him, has left no deficiency for him in any respect.

But this human, due to being far from the light of faith and discipline, steps onto the path of rebellion, injustice, and oppression, and occupies himself with ingratitude for blessings:

Monopolists try to restrict the vast Divine blessings to themselves and take control of vital resources. Even though they themselves can only consume a little, they deprive others of reaching them.

These injustices, which appear in the form of monopolization, colonialism, and violation of the rights of others, subject his peaceful living environment to storms, create wars, shed blood, and lead wealth and souls to destruction.

In truth, the Quran says: O man, everything is available to you in sufficient measure, provided that you are not unjust and ungrateful, that you be content with your right, and do not violate the rights of others!

[1]: Of course, today's scientists attribute other movements to the sun, such as rotating around itself or moving along with the entire solar system within the heart of the galaxy in which it is located.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ رَبِّ ٱجْعَلْ هَـٰذَا ٱلْبَلَدَ ءَامِنًا وَٱجْنُبْنِى وَبَنِىَّ أَن نَّعْبُدَ ٱلْأَصْنَامَ (٣٥)
When Abraham said, ‘My Lord! Make this city a sanctuary, and save me and my children from worshiping idols. (35)
رَبِّ إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۖ فَمَن تَبِعَنِى فَإِنَّهُۥ مِنِّى ۖ وَمَنْ عَصَانِى فَإِنَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٣٦)

رَبَّنَآ إِنَّكَ تَعْلَمُ مَا نُخْفِى وَمَا نُعْلِنُ ۗ وَمَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ (٣٨)
