Commentary

The Constructive Prayers of Abraham, the Destroyer of Idols

Since the preceding verses discussed the true believers and those grateful for Allah’s blessings, the verses under discussion present a portion of the prayers and requests of Abraham, the steadfast and grateful servant of God. This serves to complete the previous discussion and provide a model and pattern for those who wish to make the best use of Divine blessings.

First, it says: "And remember when Abraham said to the presence of God: 'My Lord, make this city (Mecca) a land of peace and security'" (وَ إِذْ قالَ إِبْراهِيمُ رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هَذَا الْبَلَدَ آمِناً).

"And include me and my children in Your grace and favor, and keep us away from worshiping idols" (وَ اجْنُبْنِي وَ بَنِيَّ أَنْ نَعْبُدَ الْأَصْنامَ‌).

"For I know what a great and devastating calamity idol-worship is, and I have seen with my own eyes the victims of this path: 'My Lord, indeed these idols have led many of mankind astray'" (رَبِّ إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ كَثِيراً مِنَ النَّاسِ‌).

And what a dangerous misguidance it is, where they have lost everything, even their intellect and wisdom, in this pursuit.

"My God, I call towards Your Monotheism (Tawhid) and invite everyone to You; 'So whoever follows me, he is of me, and whoever disobeys me—if he is capable of guidance and forgiveness—then show him affection, for indeed You are Oft-Forgiving, Most Merciful'" (فَمَنْ تَبِعَنِي فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي وَ مَنْ عَصانِي فَإِنَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

In truth, with this expression, Abraham wishes to convey to the presence of Allah that even if my own children deviate from the path of Monotheism and turn toward idols, they are not of me; and if strangers follow this line, they are like my children and brothers.

This polite and deeply affectionate expression of Abraham is also noteworthy; he does not say, "whoever disobeys me is not of me, so punish him in such and such a way," but rather says, "whoever disobeys me, You are Oft-Forgiving, Most Merciful!"

Then he continues his prayer and supplication as follows: "Our Lord! I have settled some of my offspring in a valley without any cultivation or water or plants, near Your Sacred House, so that they may establish prayer" (رَبَّنا إِنِّي أَسْكَنْتُ مِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِي بِوادٍ غَيْرِ ذِي زَرْعٍ عِنْدَ بَيْتِكَ الْمُحَرَّمِ رَبَّنا لِيُقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ).

This occurred when, after Allah granted Abraham a son named "Ishmael" from his handmaid "Hagar," the jealousy of his first wife "Sarah" was stirred, and she could not bear the presence of Hagar and her child. She asked Abraham to take the mother and child to another location, and Abraham, following Allah's command, submitted to this request.

He brought Ishmael and his mother Hagar to the land of Mecca—which at that time was a dry, silent land devoid of everything—and left them there, bid them farewell, and departed.

It was not long before the child and the mother became thirsty under that hot and scorching sun. Hagar made great efforts to save her child's life, but Allah, who had willed that this land become a great center of worship, made the spring of Zamzam appear. Soon, the nomadic tribe of "Jurhum," passing nearby, became aware of the situation and settled there, and "Mecca" gradually took shape.

Then Abraham continues his prayer as follows:

"O Allah! Now that they have settled in this scorching desert out of respect for Your Great House, incline the hearts of a group of people toward them and place their love in their hearts" (فَاجْعَلْ أَفْئِدَةً مِنَ النَّاسِ تَهْوِي إِلَيْهِمْ‌).

"And provide them with various fruits (material and spiritual fruits), that they may perhaps give thanks for Your blessings" (وَ ارْزُقْهُمْ مِنَ الثَّمَراتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَشْكُرُونَ‌).

And since a monotheistic and aware person knows that his knowledge is limited compared to the knowledge of Allah, and that only Allah knows what is in his best interest—for he might ask Allah for something that is not good for him, or not ask for things that are—and sometimes there are matters within his soul that he cannot express in words, he thus says following his previous requests:

"Our Lord! You know well what we conceal and what we reveal" (رَبَّنا إِنَّكَ تَعْلَمُ ما نُخْفِي وَ ما نُعْلِنُ‌).

"And nothing is hidden from Allah in the earth or in the heaven" (وَ ما يَخْفى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ لا فِي السَّماءِ).

If I am saddened by the separation from my son and wife, You know; and if my tears flow openly from my eyes, You see.

If the sorrow of separation has surrounded my heart, yet is mingled with the joy of fulfilling my duty and obeying Your command, You still know.

And if, at the time of parting, my wife says to me: "To whom are you entrusting me?" You are aware of all this, and the future of this land and the future of those whose lives are tightly intertwined are all clear before Your knowledge.

Then he refers to giving thanks for Allah's blessings—one of the most important of which for Abraham was becoming the father of two noble sons, Ishmael and Isaac, in his old age—saying: "Praise be to Allah, who has granted me Ishmael and Isaac in my old age," and has answered my prayer (الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي وَهَبَ لِي عَلَى الْكِبَرِ إِسْماعِيلَ وَ إِسْحاقَ‌) [1].

[1]: There is discussion among commentators regarding Abraham's age at the birth of his sons Ishmael and Isaac: some have said Abraham was 99 years old when his first son Ishmael was born, and 112 years old when Isaac came into the world; some have written less than that, and some more. What is certain is that he was at an age where, habitually, the birth of a child seemed very unlikely.

"Indeed, my Lord is surely the Hearer of prayer" (إِنَّ رَبِّي لَسَمِيعُ الدُّعاءِ).

And again he continues his request, supplication, and prayer, saying: "My Lord, make me an establisher of prayer, and from my offspring as well, O my God" (رَبِّ اجْعَلْنِي مُقِيمَ الصَّلاةِ وَ مِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِي‌). "Our Lord, and accept my prayer" (رَبَّنا وَ تَقَبَّلْ دُعاءِ).

And the final request and prayer of Abraham here is: "Our Lord, forgive me and my parents and all the believers on the Day the account is established" (رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لِي وَ لِوالِدَيَّ وَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْحِسابُ‌).

Key Points

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 10; p. 365

1- Was Mecca a City at That Time?

In the above verses, we saw that Abraham once says, "O Allah, I have settled my offspring in a land devoid of water, habitation, and cultivation." This certainly refers to the beginning of his arrival in the land of Mecca, when there was neither water nor habitation, nor houses nor inhabitants; only the remains of the House of Allah were visible there, along with a cluster of dry, waterless, and barren mountains. However, we know that this was not Abraham's only journey to the land of Mecca; rather, he repeatedly stepped into this holy land while "Mecca" was gradually taking the form of a city, the tribe of "Jurhum" had settled there, and the emergence of the Zamzam spring had made it habitable.

It seems that these prayers of Abraham were during one of these later journeys, and therefore he says, "O Allah, make this 'city' a place of peace and security."

And if he speaks of a "valley without cultivation," he is either reporting from the past and recounting the memory of his first journey, or referring to the fact that the land of Mecca, even after becoming a city, is a land unsuitable for cultivation whose needs must be brought from outside, because geographically it is situated among a cluster of dry mountains with very little water.

2- The Security of the Land of Mecca:

It is interesting that the first request Abraham made to Allah in this land was for "security." This shows that the blessing of security is the first condition for human life and settlement in a region, and for any kind of development, habitation, progress, and advancement; and truly it is so.

If a place is not secure, it is not habitable, even if all the blessings of the world are gathered there. Fundamentally, a city, homeland, or country that lacks the blessing of security will lose all other blessings!

Here, one must also note that Allah answered Abraham's prayer regarding the security of Mecca in two ways: He gave it both "existential security" (takwini), as it became a city that has seen fewer insecurity-causing events throughout history, and "legislative security" (tashri'i), meaning Allah commanded that all humans and even animals be safe and secure in this land.

Hunting its animals is forbidden, and even pursuing criminals who take refuge in this Sanctuary and the House of the Kaaba is not permissible; one can only cut off provisions to such criminals to compel them to come out and surrender for the execution of justice.

3- Why Does Abraham Request Distance from Idol-Worship?

There is no doubt that Abraham was an infallible Prophet, and his immediate children—who are certainly included in the word "bani" (sons) in the above verse, namely Ishmael and Isaac—were also infallible Prophets. Yet, despite this, he requests: "O Allah, keep me and them away from worshiping idols!"

This is evidence of the extreme emphasis on the issue of fighting idol-worship, such that even infallible and idol-breaking Prophets make such a request to Allah. This is exactly like the Prophet's emphasis in his testaments to Ali عليه السلام—or other Imams to their successors—regarding prayer, where the possibility of them abandoning it was never conceivable; rather, fundamentally, prayer was established through their efforts and struggles.

Now this "Question" arises: How did Abraham say, "My Lord, indeed these idols have led many of mankind astray," while they were nothing more than stone and wood and had no power to lead people astray?

The answer to this question can be found here: Firstly, idols were not always made of stone and wood; sometimes Pharaohs and Nimrods invited people to worship them, calling themselves the "Most High Lord" and the "Giver of life and death."

Secondly, the stone and wooden idols were so adorned by their custodians and directors, and such ceremonies were held for them, that they were truly misleading for the simple-minded masses.

4- Who are the Followers of Abraham?

In the above verses, we read that Abraham says: "O Allah, those who follow me are of me." Were the followers of Abraham only those who were in his creed and religion during his era or subsequent eras, or does it include all monotheists and God-worshipers of the world—by virtue of Abraham being the symbol of Monotheism and idol-breaking?

From the verses of the Quran—where it introduces the religion of Islam as the creed and religion of Abraham [1]—it is clearly understood that Abraham's prayer includes all monotheists and fighters in the path of Monotheism.

In the narrations that have reached us from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, this interpretation is confirmed.

Among them, in a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever loves us is of us, the Ahl al-Bayt." I said: "May I be sacrificed for you, from you?!" He said: "From us, by Allah. Have you not heard the words of Abraham: 'So whoever follows me, he is of me'?" [2] This hadith shows that following the school of thought and the connection of programs causes one to enter the family in a spiritual sense.

In another hadith from Imam Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام, we read that he said: "We are the family of Abraham. Do you then turn away from the religion of Abraham while Allah the Almighty has said (quoting Abraham): 'So whoever follows me, he is of me'?"

[1]: Surah Al-Hajj, Verse 78. [2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 548.

5- The Valley Without Cultivation and the Secure Sanctuary of Allah

Those who have gone to Mecca know well that the House of Allah, Masjid al-Haram, and Mecca in general are situated amidst a cluster of dry, waterless, and barren mountains; it is as if the rocks were first roasted in a hot oven and then fixed in their places.

At the same time, this dry and scorching land is the greatest center of worship and the oldest focus of Monotheism on earth. Furthermore, it is the Secure Sanctuary of Allah, and as we said, it possesses both existential and legislative security.

Here, the question arises for many: Why did Allah place such an important center in such a land?

Ali عليه السلام, in the "Khutbah al-Qasi'ah," has explained the philosophy of this choice with the most eloquent expressions and beautiful terms:

"He placed it in the most rugged parts of the earth's lands, with the least soil... between harsh mountains and soft sands... and had He, the Sublime, willed to place His Sacred House and His great sites of worship among gardens and rivers, and level and stable ground, abundant with trees, with low-hanging fruits, dense buildings, and connected villages, amidst golden wheat and green meadows, and surrounded by fertile lands, lush courtyards, blooming gardens, and flourishing roads, then the magnitude of the reward would have decreased according to the lightness of the trial. And if the foundation upon which it is carried and the stones with which it is raised were of green emerald, red ruby, and light and radiance, that would have lessened the struggle of doubt in the chests, and would have removed the striving against Iblis from the hearts, and would have dismissed the surging of uncertainty from the people. But Allah tests His servants with various hardships, and makes them worship through various struggles, and tries them with sorts of difficulties, to extract arrogance from their hearts, and to settle humility in their souls, and to make that open doors to His grace and easy means for His forgiveness." [1]

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 192 (al-Qasi'ah).

6- The Seven Requests of Abraham

In the verses that passed, Abraham—the hero of Monotheism, supplication, and the struggle against idols, idol-worshipers, and oppressors—makes seven requests from Allah, the first of which is the request for the security of the city of Mecca, that great center of the monotheistic society (and how meaningful this request is).

His second request is to remain distant from idol-worship, which encompasses the foundation and basis of all religious beliefs and programs.

His third request is for the inclination of hearts and the attention of the public opinion of the masses of God-worshipers—which is the greatest asset of a person in society—toward his children and the followers of his school of thought.

The fourth request is to benefit from various fruits, as a prelude to gratitude and greater attention to the Creator of those blessings.

His fifth request is for success in establishing prayer, which is the greatest bond between man and Allah; he makes this request not only for himself but for his children as well.

Abraham's sixth wish is the acceptance of his prayer, and we know that Allah accepts a prayer that arises from a pure heart and an untainted soul; in reality, this request implicitly means a request for the success of keeping the heart and soul pure from any taint.

And finally, his seventh and last request is that if any slip has occurred from him, the Oft-Forgiving and Merciful Lord include him in His grace and forgiveness, and likewise benefit his parents and all believers from this grace and mercy on the Day of Resurrection.

In this way, Abraham's seven requests begin with security and end with forgiveness. It is interesting that he seeks these not only for himself but also for others, for the men of God have never been and will never be "exclusivists"!

7- Does Abraham Pray for His Father?

Undoubtedly, "Azar" was an idol-worshiper, and as the Quran says, Abraham's efforts to guide him were not effective. If we accept that Azar was Abraham's father, the question arises as to why Abraham requested forgiveness for him in the above verses, while the Quran explicitly forbids believers from seeking forgiveness for polytheists (Surah At-Tawbah, Verse 113).

From this, it becomes clear that "Azar" cannot be considered Abraham's father, and the statement that the word "Ab" in the Arabic language is sometimes applied to an "uncle" is completely acceptable in light of the verses under discussion.

In short, "Ab" and "Walid" are different in the Arabic language; the word "Walid," which is also used in the above verses, refers exclusively to the biological father, whereas the word "Ab," used in the case of "Azar," can mean uncle.

By combining the above verses with the verses in Surah At-Tawbah regarding the prohibition of seeking forgiveness for polytheists, we conclude that "Azar" was certainly not Abraham's father. [1]

[1]: For further explanation, refer to Volume 5 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 303, and Volume 6, page 160.

Ayahs

وَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَـٰفِلًا عَمَّا يَعْمَلُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ ۚ إِنَّمَا يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ لِيَوْمٍ تَشْخَصُ فِيهِ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ (٤٢)
Do not suppose that Allah is oblivious of what the wrongdoers are doing. He is only granting them respite until the day when the eyes will be glazed. (42)
مُهْطِعِينَ مُقْنِعِى رُءُوسِهِمْ لَا يَرْتَدُّ إِلَيْهِمْ طَرْفُهُمْ ۖ وَأَفْـِٔدَتُهُمْ هَوَآءٌ (٤٣)
Scrambling with their heads upturned, there will be a fixed gaze in their eyes, and their hearts will be vacant. (43)
وَأَنذِرِ ٱلنَّاسَ يَوْمَ يَأْتِيهِمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ فَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ أَخِّرْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ نُّجِبْ دَعْوَتَكَ وَنَتَّبِعِ ٱلرُّسُلَ ۗ أَوَلَمْ تَكُونُوٓا۟ أَقْسَمْتُم مِّن قَبْلُ مَا لَكُم مِّن زَوَالٍ (٤٤)
Warn the people of the day when the punishment will overtake them, whereat the wrongdoers will say, ‘Our Lord! Respite us for a short time so that we may respond to Your call and follow the apostles.’ [They will be told,] ‘Did you not use to swear earlier that there would be no reverse for you, (44)
وَسَكَنتُمْ فِى مَسَـٰكِنِ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَتَبَيَّنَ لَكُمْ كَيْفَ فَعَلْنَا بِهِمْ وَضَرَبْنَا لَكُمُ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ (٤٥)
while you dwelt in the dwellings of those who had wronged themselves [before], and it had been made clear to you how We had dealt with them [before you], and We had [also] cited examples for you?’ (45)

Commentary

The Day When Eyes Freeze!

Since the previous verses mentioned the "Day of Reckoning," the verses under discussion depict the state of the wrongdoers and oppressors on that day—a depiction that is shaking and awakening. Furthermore, by explaining this part of the issues regarding the Resurrection, the preceding sections on Monotheism (Tawhid) are completed.

First, it begins with a threatening tone (a threat directed at the wrongdoers and oppressors): O Prophet! "Do not ever think that Allah is heedless of what the wrongdoers do" (وَ لا تَحْسَبَنَّ اللَّهَ غافِلًا عَمَّا يَعْمَلُ الظَّالِمُونَ‌).

This statement is, in fact, an answer to the question of those who ask: If this world has a God—a God who is Just and Fair—then why has He left the oppressors to their own devices? Is He heedless of their state, or does He know but lacks the power to prevent them?! In response to this question, the Quran says: Allah is never heedless. If He does not punish them immediately, it is because this world is an arena and a place for the testing and cultivation of human beings, and this goal is impossible without freedom; however, their day of reckoning will eventually arrive.

Then it says: "Allah only delays [their punishment] until a Day when eyes will stare [in horror]" and become fixed on a single point, remaining motionless (إِنَّما يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ لِيَوْمٍ تَشْخَصُ فِيهِ الْأَبْصارُ).

"The punishments of that day are so terrifying that these oppressors, out of extreme horror, will crane their necks, raising their heads toward the sky, and even their eyelids will remain motionless, and their hearts will become entirely empty due to extreme anxiety and distress" (مُهْطِعِينَ مُقْنِعِي رُؤُسِهِمْ لا يَرْتَدُّ إِلَيْهِمْ طَرْفُهُمْ وَ أَفْئِدَتُهُمْ هَواءٌ).

"Tashkhas" (تشخص) is derived from the root "shukhus" (شخوص), meaning the eyes ceasing to move and staring fixedly at a point.

"Muhti'in" (مهطعين) is from the root "ihta'" (اهطاع), meaning "to crane the neck." Some have interpreted it as "speeding," and others as "looking with humiliation and humility," but considering the other phrases in the verse, the first meaning seems more appropriate.

"Muqni'i" (مقنعی) is from the root "iqna'" (اقناع), meaning "to raise the head toward the sky."

The phrase "their gaze does not return to them" (لا يَرْتَدُّ إِلَيْهِمْ طَرْفُهُمْ‌) means that their eyelids do not blink out of horror and terror, as if they have ceased to function like the eyes of the dead! The phrase "their hearts are void" (أَفْئِدَتُهُمْ هَواءٌ) means the emptying of their hearts, exactly like what we say in Persian: "So-and-so gave me such terrifying news that my heart suddenly went empty," or "my heart sank." In reality, they become so panicked that they forget everything, even themselves, as if all knowledge has fled from their hearts and souls, and they have lost all strength and power.

The mention of these five attributes: the staring of the eyes, the craning of the necks, the raising of the heads, the cessation of eyelid movement, and the forgetting of all thoughts, is a very vivid portrayal of the extraordinarily intense horror and terror that will seize the wrongdoers on that day. Those who always ridiculed everything with their proud and arrogant gazes will become so wretched that day that they will even lose the ability to close their eyelids.

To avoid seeing those horrific scenes, they only fix their eyes staring toward the sky, because whichever way they look, a terrifying sight is before their eyes.

Those who considered themselves "the ultimate intellects" and regarded others as mindless will lose their reason and intelligence to such an extent that their gaze will be that of the insane, or rather, the dead—a dry, indifferent, motionless gaze full of fear and horror! Truly, when the Quran wishes to visualize a scene, it provides the most complete depictions in the shortest phrases, an example of which is the short verse above.

Then, so that it is not imagined that divine punishments pertain only to a specific group, He says to His Prophet as a general command: "Warn all people of the Day when the painful punishment of your Lord comes to the evildoers. When the wrongdoers see the horrific results of their deeds, they will regret and think of making amends, saying: Our Lord, give us respite for a short term" (وَ أَنْذِرِ النَّاسَ يَوْمَ يَأْتِيهِمُ الْعَذابُ فَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا رَبَّنا أَخِّرْنا إِلى‌ أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ‌). So that we may use this short respite to "respond to Your call and follow the messengers" (نُجِبْ دَعْوَتَكَ وَ نَتَّبِعِ الرُّسُلَ‌).

But they are immediately rejected, and this bone-shaking message is given to them: Such a thing is impossible; the time for action has ended. "Had you not sworn before that there would be no decline or end for your life of power?" (أَ وَ لَمْ تَكُونُوا أَقْسَمْتُمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ زَوالٍ‌).

"Were you not the ones who dwelt in the palaces and homes and dwellings of those who had wronged themselves?" (وَ سَكَنْتُمْ فِي مَساكِنِ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ‌). "And it had become clearly manifest to you how We dealt with them" (وَ تَبَيَّنَ لَكُمْ كَيْفَ فَعَلْنا بِهِمْ‌).

"And We set forth for you many shaking examples from the states of previous nations" (وَ ضَرَبْنا لَكُمُ الْأَمْثالَ‌).

But none of these lessons of warning affected you, and you continued in your disgraceful deeds and oppression. And now that you are caught in the grip of divine retribution, you request an extension of time and a continuation of respite? What extension? And what respite?

Whatever there was has ended!

Key Points

1- Why is the Prophet the addressee here?

There is no doubt that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله never imagined that Allah is heedless of the deeds of the wrongdoers; nevertheless, in the above verses, the speech is directed at the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "Do not think that Allah is heedless of the deeds of the oppressors."

This is, in fact, a case of delivering a message indirectly to others, which is one of the techniques of eloquence—where sometimes an individual is addressed, but the intention is someone else or others.

Furthermore, this expression is essentially an allusion to a threat, just as we might even say to a guilty person, "Do not think I have forgotten your offenses," meaning I will settle the account with you in due time!

In any case, the foundation of life in this world is that all individuals are given sufficient respite to bring out what is within them, and the arena of the test of perfection is expanded sufficiently so that no excuse or pretext remains for anyone, and the possibility of return, reform, and compensation is given to all. The respite given to sinners is for this very reason.

2- What day is "the Day when the punishment comes to them"?

In the above verses, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is commanded to warn the people of the day when divine punishment comes to them.

Regarding which day is meant by this, commentators have offered three possibilities:

First, that it is the Day of Judgment and Resurrection.

Second, that it is the day of the arrival of death, as the prelude to divine punishments comes to the wrongdoers starting from that day.

Third, that it refers to the day of the descent of certain worldly calamities and punishments, such as the punishments that descended upon the people of Lot, the people of 'Ad and Thamud, the people of Noah, and the people of Pharaoh, who perished amidst storms and surging waves of the sea, or in earthquakes, or by means of fierce and destructive gales.

Although many commentators prefer the first possibility, the phrases that follow it strongly support the third possibility and show that the intention is destructive worldly punishments. This is because following this phrase, we read: Upon witnessing the signs of punishment, the wrongdoers say, "Our Lord, give us a short respite to make amends."

The expression "akhkhirna" (delay us) is a clear indication of a request for the continuation of life in this world. If they had said this on the Day of Resurrection upon seeing the signs of punishment, they would have had to say: "O Allah, return us to the world," just as we read in Surah Al-An'am, verse 27: "If you could but see when they are made to stand before the Fire and will say, 'Oh, would that we could be sent back [to the world] and not deny the signs of our Lord and be among the believers!'" (وَ لَوْ تَرى‌ إِذْ وُقِفُوا عَلَى النَّارِ فَقالُوا يا لَيْتَنا نُرَدُّ وَ لا نُكَذِّبَ بِآياتِ رَبِّنا وَ نَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

Immediately in the following verse, their answer is given as such: "...But if they were returned, they would certainly revert to that which they were forbidden; and indeed, they are liars" (وَ لَوْ رُدُّوا لَعادُوا لِما نُهُوا عَنْهُ وَ إِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ‌).

Here, the question arises: if this verse is a warning of worldly punishment, and the previous verse (Do not think Allah is heedless...) was a warning of the punishment of the Hereafter, how are they consistent with each other, especially since the word "innama" (only) is evidence that their punishment will only be in the Resurrection and not in this world?

However, by considering one point, the answer to this question becomes clear: the punishment in which no change or alteration can occur is the punishment of the Resurrection, which encompasses all wrongdoers. But worldly punishments, in addition to not being universal, are reversible. It is also necessary to mention that regarding destructive worldly punishments—like the painful punishments that overtook the people of Noah, the people of Pharaoh, and their like—once they begin, the doors of repentance are completely closed, and there is no way back. This is because most sinners, when faced with such punishments, express regret, and in reality, a kind of forced state of remorse seizes them which is worthless. Therefore, they must seek to make amends before these punishments occur and begin. [1]

3- Why is the request for respite not accepted?

In various verses of the Holy Quran, we read that evildoers and wrongdoers, at different stages, request to return to life to make up for their past.

Some of these verses relate to the Day of Judgment and Resurrection, such as verse 28 of Surah Al-An'am mentioned above.

Others relate to the arrival of the time of death, such as verse 99 of Surah Al-Mu'minun, which says: "Until, when death comes to one of them, he says, 'My Lord, send me back that I might do righteousness in that which I left behind'" (حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قالَ رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ لَعَلِّي أَعْمَلُ صالِحاً فِيما تَرَكْتُ‌).

And sometimes it is mentioned regarding the descent of destructive punishments, such as the verses under discussion, which say that upon the descent of punishment, the wrongdoers request an extension of time and a continuation of respite.

But interestingly, in all these cases, a negative response is given to them.

The reason for this is also clear, because none of these requests are genuine or serious. These are reactions to that state of desperation and extraordinary distress that arises even in the worst of people, and they are never evidence of an internal transformation, a spiritual revolution, or a real decision to change the course of one's life.

[1]: For further explanation, refer to Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 315 onwards, under verse 18 of Surah An-Nisa.

This is exactly like the state of the polytheists who, when caught in the terrifying whirlpools of the seas, would call upon Allah sincerely, but as soon as the storm subsided and they reached the shore of safety, they would forget everything! Therefore, the Quran explicitly states in some of the verses mentioned above: "But if they were returned, they would certainly revert to that which they were forbidden" (وَ لَوْ رُدُّوا لَعادُوا لِما نُهُوا عَنْهُ‌). If these people were to return to normal life once more, they would continue the same program; as the saying goes, it would be "the same old story." No change would appear in their conduct.

Ayahs

وَقَدْ مَكَرُوا۟ مَكْرَهُمْ وَعِندَ ٱللَّهِ مَكْرُهُمْ وَإِن كَانَ مَكْرُهُمْ لِتَزُولَ مِنْهُ ٱلْجِبَالُ (٤٦)
They certainly devised their plots, but their plots are known to Allah, and their plots are not such as to dislodge the mountains. (46)
فَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِفَ وَعْدِهِۦ رُسُلَهُۥٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ ذُو ٱنتِقَامٍ (٤٧)
So do not suppose that Allah will break His promise to His apostles. Indeed Allah is all-mighty, avenger. (47)
يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ ٱلْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ ۖ وَبَرَزُوا۟ لِلَّهِ ٱلْوَٰحِدِ ٱلْقَهَّارِ (٤٨)
The day when the earth is turned into another earth and the heavens [as well], and they are presented before Allah, the One, the All-paramount (48)
وَتَرَى ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ يَوْمَئِذٍ مُّقَرَّنِينَ فِى ٱلْأَصْفَادِ (٤٩)
—on that day you will see the guilty bound together in chains, (49)
سَرَابِيلُهُم مِّن قَطِرَانٍ وَتَغْشَىٰ وُجُوهَهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ (٥٠)
their garments made of pitch, and the Fire covering their faces, (50)
لِيَجْزِىَ ٱللَّهُ كُلَّ نَفْسٍ مَّا كَسَبَتْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٥١)
so that Allah may reward every soul for what it has earned. Indeed Allah is swift at reckoning. (51)
هَـٰذَا بَلَـٰغٌ لِّلنَّاسِ وَلِيُنذَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَلِيَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَا هُوَ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ وَلِيَذَّكَّرَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (٥٢)
This is a proclamation for mankind, so that they may be warned thereby and know that He is indeed the One God, and those who possess intellect may take admonition. (52)

Commentary

The Plots of the Oppressors Lead Nowhere!

In the preceding verses, some of the punishments for the oppressors were mentioned. These verses first point to a portion of their deeds and then describe another part of their severe and painful retribution.

The first verse says: "They indeed devised their plots, and they schemed and acted with mischief to the extent of their power" (وَ قَدْ مَكَرُوا مَكْرَهُمْ).

In short, there was no action the enemies of the Prophet did not take to erase and destroy Islam—ranging from enticements and threats to harassment, assassination plots, spreading rumors, and leveling various accusations.

Yet, despite all this, "Allah is fully aware of all their schemes, and all their deeds are recorded with Him" (وَ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ مَكْرُهُمْ).

In any case, do not be concerned; these deceptions, plans, and designs of theirs will have no effect on you, "even if their plots were such that mountains would be moved thereby" (وَ إِنْ كانَ مَكْرُهُمْ لِتَزُولَ مِنْهُ الْجِبالُ).

"Makar" (plot/scheme)—as we have previously noted—means any kind of devising of a solution or plan. Sometimes it is coupled with sabotage and corruption, and sometimes without it (although in modern Persian, makar is used in the first sense, in Arabic literature its concept is broader; hence, this word is sometimes attributed to Allah as well).

Regarding the interpretation of the phrase "عِنْدَ اللَّهِ مَكْرُهُمْ" (their plot is with Allah), two possibilities have been suggested:

Some commentators, such as Allamah Tabataba'i in Al-Mizan, have said the meaning of this phrase is that Allah has complete encompassment over all their plans, designs, and deceptions.

Others, like the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, have said the meaning is that the recompense for their plot is fixed with Allah (thus, the phrase implies a hidden word: with Allah is the [recompense of] their plot).

However, the first meaning is undoubtedly more accurate, as it aligns with the literal appearance of the verse and requires no assumed omissions.

The subsequent phrase, which says "even if their plot were to move mountains," reinforces this interpretation. That is, no matter how strong and capable they are at plotting, Allah is more knowledgeable and more powerful than them: He shatters their conspiracies.

Once again, the discourse turns to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), serving as a threat to the oppressors and evildoers, saying: "Do not think that Allah will fail in His promise to His messengers" (فَلا تَحْسَبَنَّ اللَّهَ مُخْلِفَ وَعْدِهِ رُسُلَهُ). This is because failure to keep a promise stems from either a lack of power or because retribution and vengeance are not in one's nature; however, "Allah is Almighty and Capable of Retribution" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ ذُو انتِقامٍ).

This verse is, in fact, a complement to the verse we had earlier: (وَ لا تَحْسَبَنَّ اللَّهَ غافِلًا عَمَّا يَعْمَلُ الظَّالِمُونَ) "And do not think that Allah is heedless of what the oppressors do."

Meaning, if you see that the oppressors and tyrants have been given a respite, it is neither because of the Lord's heedlessness of their actions nor because He will fail in His promise. Rather, all their accounts will be settled one day, and He will give them their just punishment.

Incidentally, the word "intiqam" (retribution/vengeance), which in modern Persian usage implies "retaliation coupled with spite and a lack of forgiveness," does not originally carry this meaning. Rather, the concept of intiqam is simply to penalize and punish—a punishment that, in the case of Allah, is consistent with merit and justice, or rather, is the result of man's own deeds. It goes without saying that if Allah did not possess such retribution, it would be contrary to Wisdom and Justice.

Then He adds: "This punishment will be on the day when this earth is transformed into another earth, and the heavens into other heavens" (يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ وَ السَّماواتُ).

On that day, everything will be made new after its destruction, and man will step into a new world with fresh conditions—a world where everything is different from this one: its vastness, its blessings, and its punishments. "And on that day, everyone, with all they possess and their entire being, shall appear before Allah, the One, the Subduer" (وَ بَرَزُوا لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ).

"Buruz" (appearing/emerging) is originally derived from the root baraz, which means a vast space or area. The word buruz itself means to be placed in such a vast space, the necessary consequence of which is becoming manifest and visible. For this reason, buruz is often used to mean "manifestation" (take note).

Regarding what it means for humans to "appear" before Allah on the Day of Resurrection, commentators have offered several explanations:

Many have interpreted it as emerging from the graves.

However, it is possible that buruz means the manifestation of the entirety of man's being—both his inner and outer self—in that arena. As we read in Surah Ghafir, verse 16: (يَوْمَ هُمْ بارِزُونَ لا يَخْفى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْ‌ءٌ) "The Day they shall come forth, nothing of them being hidden from Allah." And in Surah At-Tariq, verse 9: (يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرائِرُ) "The Day when secrets are laid bare."

In any case, describing Allah in this state as "The Subduer" (Al-Qahhar) is evidence of His mastery over all things and His dominance over the inner and outer states of everyone.

A question arises here: Is anything in this world hidden from Allah that it should become manifest there? Is Allah unaware of the existence of the dead in their graves, or does He not know the inner secrets of humans?

The answer to this question becomes clear by noting one point: in this world, we have an "outward" and an "inward," and sometimes due to our limited knowledge, the misconception arises that Allah does not see our inner selves. But in the other world, everything becomes so sunlit and manifest that there will be no "outward" or "inward." Everything is apparent, and even the possibility does not occur to anyone's heart that something might have remained hidden from Allah.

In other words, the expression of "appearing" and "manifesting" is in comparison to our perception, not in comparison to Allah's knowledge.

In the next verse, He depicts the state of the criminals in another way: "On that day, you will see the criminals bound together in shackles—shackles that bind their hands to their necks and then link them to one another" (وَ تَرَى الْمُجْرِمِينَ يَوْمَئِذٍ مُقَرَّنِينَ فِي الْأَصْفادِ).

"Asfad" is the plural of safad or sifad, which originally means a shackle or fetter. Some have said it specifically refers to the chain that binds the hands and neck together.

"Muqarranin" is derived from the root qarn and iqtiran, meaning to be joined or linked. However, when moved to the taf'il form, it implies "multiplicity" or "intensity." Therefore, taken together, muqarranin refers to those who are brought very close to one another.

Regarding who is meant by this word in the above verse, commentators have mentioned three interpretations:

First, that the criminals on that day will be bound together with shackles in a long chain and will appear in the arena of Resurrection in this manner. These shackles are a physical manifestation of the practical and ideological bond of these sinners in this world, where they joined hands, rushed to each other's aid, and maintained relationships and cooperation in the path of oppression and corruption. This connection is manifested there in the form of chains that link them to one another.

Second, that the criminals on that day will be joined with devils by means of chains, and their inner bond in this world will be manifested as being chained together in the next world.

Third, that their hands will be joined to their necks by means of chains.

There is no obstacle to all these meanings being true regarding the criminals, although the literal appearance of the verse more strongly suggests the first meaning.

Then He describes their clothing, which is itself a great torment for them, saying: "Their shirts shall be of pitch (liquid sulfur), and the fire shall envelop their faces" (سَرابِيلُهُمْ مِنْ قَطِرانٍ وَ تَغْشى‌ وُجُوهَهُمُ النَّارُ).

"Sarabīl" is the plural of sirbāl, meaning a shirt, regardless of the material. Some have said it means any type of clothing, but the first meaning is more famous.

"Qatiran" (pitch/tar)—which is sometimes read with a fatha on the qaf and sukun on the ta, or kasra on the qaf—refers to a substance extracted from a tree called Abhal, which is boiled until it thickens. It was rubbed on the bodies of camels suffering from "mange" (jarab), and it was believed that its burning sensation would destroy the disease. In any case, it is a black, foul-smelling, and highly flammable substance [^1] [^2].

Thus, the concept of the phrase "سَرابِيلُهُمْ مِنْ قَطِرانٍ" is that instead of clothes, their bodies will be covered with a type of black, foul-smelling, flammable substance—a garment that is ugly, malodorous, and itself capable of burning and flaming. Possessing these four defects, it is considered the worst of clothing, for clothing is worn to be an adornment and to protect man from heat and cold; this clothing, contrary to all others, is both hideous and burning!

It is also noteworthy that by dressing in the "garment of sin" in this world, the criminals both blacken their own faces before Allah and the stench of their sin pollutes society. Their deeds also cause the fire of corruption to flare up within themselves and the society they live in. This qatiran, which forms their clothing in the other world, is as if a physical manifestation of their deeds in this world.

And if we see the verse saying that flames of fire cover their faces, it is because when the garment of pitch ignites, not only the limbs but also the face—even if not coated in pitch—will burn within its flames.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 19, p. 148. [^2]: Farid Wajdi, in his Encyclopedia under the entry Qatiran, says it is a liquid obtained during the distillation of coal to obtain a specific gas, and botanical pitch is obtained from certain trees.

"These are so that Allah may recompense every soul according to what it has earned" (لِيَجْزِيَ اللَّهُ كُلَّ نَفْسٍ ما كَسَبَتْ).

Interestingly, He does not say He gives them the "recompense of their deeds." Rather, He says He will give them what they have done as a recompense. In other words, their recompense is their own manifested deeds. This verse, with this specific phrasing, is another proof for the concept of the "embodiment of deeds" (tajassum al-a'mal).

And at the end, He says: "Indeed, Allah is swift in reckoning" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ).

It is perfectly clear that when man's deeds do not perish but return to him in a transformed appearance, no reckoning could be swifter than that; in fact, his account is with him! In some narrations, we read: "Indeed, Allah Almighty reckons all creatures in the duration of a blink of an eye." Fundamentally, the Lord's reckoning does not require time, and what is mentioned in the narration is actually to indicate the shortest possible time (for further explanation, refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 2, p. 40).

Since the verses of this Surah, as well as the entirety of this Quran, serve as a call to Monotheism, a proclamation of divine laws to the people, and a warning against their transgressions, the final verse of this Surah (Surah Ibrahim) says: "This (Quran) is a public proclamation for all people" (هذا بَلاغٌ لِلنَّاسِ).

"And it is a warning for them" (وَ لِيُنْذَرُوا بِهِ), "and the goal is that they may know that He is but One God" (وَ لِيَعْلَمُوا أَنَّما هُوَ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ).

"And the purpose is that the people of understanding may take heed" (وَ لِيذَّكَّرَ أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ).

Key Points

1- The Transformation of the Earth and Heavens into Another Earth and Heaven

In the verses above, we read that on the Day of Resurrection, this earth will be transformed into another earth, and likewise the heavens into other heavens.

Is the meaning of this transformation a change of essence—meaning this earth will be completely destroyed and another earth created upon which the Resurrection will take place? Or does it mean a transformation of attributes—meaning this terrestrial globe and the heavens will be demolished, and upon their ruins, a new and fresh earth and heaven will be created, which are at a higher level of evolution compared to this earth and heaven?

The literal appearance of many Quranic verses supports the second meaning.

In Surah Al-Fajr, verse 21, we read: (كَلَّا إِذا دُكَّتِ الْأَرْضُ دَكًّا دَكًّا) "Nay! When the earth is ground to powder."

And in Surah Az-Zalzalah, which speaks of the end of the world and the beginning of Resurrection, we read: (إِذا زُلْزِلَتِ الْأَرْضُ زِلْزالَها وَ أَخْرَجَتِ الْأَرْضُ أَثْقالَها) "When the earth is shaken with its [final] earthquake, and the earth throws out its burdens."

And in Surah Al-Haqqah, verses 14 and 15: (وَ حُمِلَتِ الْأَرْضُ وَ الْجِبالُ فَدُكَّتا دَكَّةً واحِدَةً فَيَوْمَئِذٍ وَقَعَتِ الْواقِعَةُ) "And the earth and the mountains are lifted and crushed with a single blow. Then on that Day, the Occurrence will occur."

And in Surah Ta-Ha, verses 105 to 108: (وَ يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْجِبالُ فَقُلْ يَنْسِفُها رَبِّي نَسْفاً- فَيَذَرُها قاعاً صَفْصَفاً- لا تَرى‌ فِيها عِوَجاً وَ لا أَمْتاً- يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَّبِعُونَ الدَّاعِيَ لا عِوَجَ لَهُ وَ خَشَعَتِ الْأَصْواتُ لِلرَّحْمنِ فَلا تَسْمَعُ إِلَّا هَمْساً) "And they ask you about the mountains, so say, 'My Lord will blast them into dust. Then He will leave the earth as a level plain. You will not see therein a depression or an elevation.' That Day, they will follow the Caller [with] no deviation therefrom, and voices will be stilled before the Most Merciful, so you will hear nothing but a whisper."

At the beginning of Surah At-Takwir, there is also mention of the sun being extinguished, the stars darkening, and the mountains moving.

And at the beginning of Surah Al-Infitar, it speaks of the heavens being cleft asunder, the stars being scattered, and then the dead being raised from the graves! (Take note).

From the sum of these verses and their like, as well as various verses stating that humans will be raised again from the graves [^1], it is clearly understood that the current system of the world will not remain as it is, but it will not be completely annihilated either. Rather, this world will collapse, the earth will become smooth and level, and people will step onto a fresh earth (and naturally more perfect and sublime, by the rule that everything in the other world is vaster and more perfect than this world).

It is natural that our world today does not have the capacity to host the scenes of the Resurrection and is too narrow and limited for our resurrected life. As we have said many times, perhaps the relationship of that world to this world is like the relationship of this world to the confines of the fetus and the mother's womb.

Verses stating that the duration of days in the Resurrection is much longer compared to the days of this world [^1] are also good evidence of this reality.

Of course, we cannot have a precise depiction of the other world and its characteristics in this world, just as a child in the womb—even if we assume it had perfect intellect—could not perceive the characteristics of the world outside the womb.

However, we know this much: a massive transformation will occur in this world we are in; this world will be completely demolished and transformed into a completely new world. Interestingly, in several narrations cited in Islamic sources, we read that at that time, the earth and the arena of Resurrection will turn into "pure, white bread" that humans can feed upon until their accounts are settled and each moves toward their destiny.

These narrations are cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn through various chains [^2], and some Sunni commentators like Al-Qurtubi have also referred to such narrations under this verse [^3]. It is not unlikely that these narrations mean that in that world, instead of the earth being covered by soil, a nutritional substance absorbable by the human body will cover it all. In other words, soil is not something absorbable by the human body, and the nutrients in the soil must be converted in the laboratory of roots, stems, and branches of plants into substances absorbable by the human body; but on that day, instead of soil, a substance will cover the surface of the earth that is easily absorbable by the human body. If it is referred to as "bread," it is because bread constitutes the primary food (take note).

2- The Beginning and End of Surah Ibrahim

Surah Ibrahim, as we have seen, began with the statement of the Quran's sensitive role in bringing [mankind] out of the darkness of ignorance and polytheism into the light of knowledge and Monotheism, and it ends with the statement of the Quran's role in warning all the masses, teaching Monotheism, and reminding the people of understanding.

This "beginning" and "end" express the reality that everything we seek is in this very Quran. As the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, said:

"In it is the spring of the heart and the fountains of knowledge."

"The spring of hearts and the sources of sciences and knowledge are all in the Quran" [^1]. Likewise, the cure for all intellectual, moral, social, and political diseases must be sought in it:

"(Seek cure from it for your ailments)" [^2].

This statement is proof that, contrary to the practice of many Muslims today who view the Quran as a holy book revealed only for recitation and earning spiritual reward, it is a book for practical instruction throughout human life.

It is a book that grants awareness and awakens.

And finally, it is a book that both reminds the scholar and from which the masses of people draw inspiration.

Such a book must take root in the heart of Muslim life and constitute the constitution of their lives, always being a subject of discussion, research, study, and precision for better and more frequent implementation.

Forgetting this great heavenly book and turning to the deviant schools of the East and West is one of the effective factors in the backwardness, weakness, and inability of Muslims.

And how excellently Ali عليه السلام said:

"And know that no one after [possessing] the Quran has any poverty, and no one before [possessing] the Quran has any wealth." [^1]

How painful is our estrangement from the Quran, and the familiarity of strangers with the Quran.

How agonizing it is that the best means of prosperity is in our home, yet we search around the world for it.

And how tragic it is to die of thirst beside the fountain of the Water of Life, or to run after a mirage in the barren deserts?! O Allah, grant us that intellect, insight, and faith so that we do not cheaply lose this great means of prosperity, which is the blood-price of the martyrs in Your path! And grant us that alertness to know that our lost treasures are in this very great book, so that we do not stretch the hand of need toward this one or that one.

3- The First and Last Word is Monotheism

Another point that the above verses taught us is the emphasis on Monotheism (Tawhid) as the final word and the reminder to the "people of understanding" as the final admonition.

Yes, Monotheism is the most deep-rooted and profound Islamic principle, and all lines of Islamic education and training lead to it. That is, wherever we start, we must start from Monotheism, and wherever we arrive, we must end at Monotheism, for Monotheism forms the warp and woof of Islam.

Not only Monotheism in "worship" and "Godhead," but Monotheism in purpose, Monotheism in the ranks of struggle, and Monotheism in programs—all define the main foundations. Coincidentally, the great trouble of us Muslims today is that we have practically removed Monotheism from Islam.

The Arab countries, which are the birthplace of Islam, have unfortunately mostly followed the polytheistic slogans of racism, Arab glory, the life of Arabism, and Arab greatness. Other countries have each made an idol of this sort for themselves and have completely severed the bond of Islamic Monotheism that once linked the East and West of the world. They have become so self-absorbed and alienated that their fighting and quarreling with one another is more than their fighting with sworn enemies.

How shameful it is to hear that the statistics of those killed in the internal wars of Arab countries have been far more than the statistics of their victims in the struggle against Israeli Zionism.

They have such a common and dangerous enemy and yet are so scattered; alas, what would have happened if this enemy were not in the picture?! Let us speak more plainly: at the time of writing this part of the commentary, the Iraqi government, which has not yet fired a single bullet at Israeli soldiers, has so ruthlessly attacked the Islamic Republic of Iran on a minor pretext (a border dispute pretext that could certainly be resolved through negotiation) as if these two nations are neither neighbors, nor have cultural ties, nor a deep religious bond.

And on the other side, we see the common enemy (Zionism) joyfully saying: "We could not imagine a better plan than Iraq attacking Iran and both sides being severely damaged in a long war, so that our minds may be at ease for a long time!!"

It is here that it is necessary for the monotheistic, committed, and faithful Muslim to eliminate the evil of these tyrants (Taghuts) forever and send such polytheistic, hypocrisy-spreading, destructive, and enemy-pleasing governments to the depths of Hell.

End of Surah Ibrahim.

The Eventful Life of Ibrahim

The Idol-Breaking Prophet

Since this Surah is the only one in the Quran named after Ibrahim—although the accounts of Ibrahim do not appear only in this Surah, but this great divine Prophet is mentioned on various occasions in other Surahs as well—we deemed it appropriate to provide a summary of the glorious life of this hero of Monotheism at the end of this Surah. This is so that in the commentary of various verses we will encounter in the future, which require an encompassment of this Prophet's life, there will be sufficient awareness for the dear readers, and we can refer them to this discussion.

The life of Ibrahim can be presented in three distinct periods:

  1. The period before prophethood.
  2. The period of prophethood and struggle against idolaters in Babylon.
  3. The period of migration from Babylon and effort and endeavor in the lands of Egypt, Palestine, and Mecca.

The Birthplace and Infancy of Ibrahim

Ibrahim was born in the land of "Babylon," which was one of the wondrous lands of the world, ruled by a powerful yet oppressive and tyrannical government [^1].

[^1]: Some historians have considered his place of birth to be the city of "Ur" in the country of Babylon.

Ibrahim was born at a time when Nimrod bin Canaan, that tyrannical and oppressive king, ruled over Babylon and introduced himself as the great god of Babylon.

Of course, the people of Babylon did not have only this one idol; rather, they had simultaneously crafted idols of various shapes and materials and were busy worshipping them.

The government of the time, seeing idol worship as an effective means to "stupefy" and "numb" simple-minded thoughts, supported it strongly and considered any insult or disrespect to the idols a great sin and an unpardonable crime.

Historians have narrated a wondrous story about the birth of Ibrahim, the summary of which is as follows:

Astrologers had predicted the birth of a person who would struggle against the undisputed power of "Nimrod." He exerted all his efforts both to prevent the birth of such a child and to kill him should he be born.

But none of this was effective, and this infant was eventually born.

To protect him, his mother raised him in the corner of a cave near his birthplace, such that he spent thirteen years of his life there.

Finally, having been raised in that hiding place away from the eyes of Nimrod's agents and having reached adolescence, he decided to leave that sanctuary forever, step among the people, and speak to them of the lesson of Monotheism that he had discovered through inner inspiration coupled with intellectual study.

Struggle Against Various Groups of Idolaters

At this time, when the people of Babylon, in addition to the idols made by their own hands, worshipped celestial bodies such as the sun, moon, and stars, Ibrahim decided to awaken their sleeping consciences through logic and clear arguments and to remove the dark veils of false indoctrination from their pure nature (fitra), so that the light of nature might shine and they might step onto the path of Monotheism and the worship of the One.

He had studied for a long time the creation of the heavens and the earth, the power that rules over them, and their wondrous system, and the light of certainty shone in his heart (Surah Al-An'am, verse 75).

Logical Struggle with Idolaters

First, he encountered the star-worshippers and stood before a group that was busy bowing to and worshipping the star "Venus," which shines on the western horizon immediately after sunset.

Ibrahim, either out of wonder and rhetorical denial or as a way of harmonizing with the opposing side as a prelude to proving their error, called out: "Is this my lord?!" But when it set, he said: "I love not those that set."

And when the "moon" broke through the horizon and the moon-worshippers began their prayer rituals, he joined them, saying: "Is this my lord?" But when it also set, he said: "Unless my Lord guides me, I shall surely be among the people who go astray."

The "sun" tore through the dark veils of the night and cast its golden rays upon the mountains and plains. The sun-worshippers rose in prayer. Ibrahim said: "Is this my lord? This is greater!"

But when it set, he cried out: "O my people! I am indeed free from the partners you attribute to Allah! All these have a setting and a vanishing."

All these are subject to change and are prisoners in the hands of the laws of creation; they never possess will or choice of their own, let alone being the creator and sustainer of this world.

"I have turned my face toward Him who created the heavens and the earth; I am pure and steadfast in my faith in Him, and I shall never be of the polytheists" (Surah Al-An'am, verses 75 to 79).

Ibrahim left this stage of his struggle with the idolaters in the most excellent manner and was able to awaken some and at least plunge others into doubt and hesitation.

Before long, the whisper spread in that region: "Who is this youth who, with this eloquent logic and expressive speech, is finding his way into the hearts of the masses?!"

Dialogue with Azar

In another stage, Ibrahim entered into a discussion with his uncle "Azar" and, with very firm, expressive phrases coupled with affection and sometimes rebuke, warned him regarding idol worship, saying to him:

"Why do you worship that which neither hears nor sees, nor can solve any problem for you?"

"If you follow me, I will guide you to the straight path. I fear that if you follow Satan, divine punishment will seize you."

Even when his uncle, in response to these counsels, threatened to stone him, he attempted to find a way into his heavy heart with the phrase "سَلامٌ عَلَيْكَ‌" (Peace be upon you) and "I will seek forgiveness for you" (Surah Maryam, verse 47).

The Prophethood of Ibrahim

We do not have clear evidence regarding at what age Ibrahim attained the station of prophethood, but this much is understood from Surah Maryam: that he had reached the station of prophethood by the time he discussed with his uncle Azar, for we read in this Surah:

(وَ اذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ إِبْراهِيمَ إِنَّهُ كانَ صِدِّيقاً نَبِيًّا إِذْ قالَ لِأَبِيهِ يا أَبَتِ لِمَ تَعْبُدُ ما لا يَسْمَعُ وَ لا يُبْصِرُ وَ لا يُغْنِي عَنْكَ شَيْئاً) "And mention in the Book, Ibrahim. Indeed, he was a man of truth and a prophet. When he said to his father, 'O my father, why do you worship that which does not hear and does not see and will not benefit you at all?'" (Surah Maryam, verses 41 and 42). We know this event occurred before the intense confrontation with the idolaters and the story of his burning. If we add what some historians have written—that Ibrahim was 16 years old at the time of the fire story—it is proven that he had taken up this great mission from the very beginning of his youth.

Practical Struggle with Idolaters

In any case, the story of Ibrahim's confrontation with the idolaters grew more intense day by day, until it culminated in the breaking of all the idols in the temple of Babylon (with the exception of one large idol) by utilizing a perfectly suitable opportunity!

Dialogue with the Tyrannical Ruler!

The story of Ibrahim's opposition and struggle against the idols eventually reached the ears of Nimrod, and he summoned him to silence him, as he thought, through advice and counsel, or rebuke and threat.

He, who was skilled in sophistry, asked Ibrahim: "If you do not worship these idols, then who is your Lord?"

He said: "The one in whose hand is life and death."

He shouted: "O ignorant one! This is in my hand. Do you not see that I release the criminal condemned to death, and I execute the prisoner not condemned to death if I wish?!" Ibrahim, who was exceptionally skilled in crushing responses, said to him with the aid of the power of prophethood: "It is not only life and death that are in Allah's hand; the entire universe is at His command. Do you not see that in the morning the sun rises from the eastern horizon by His command and in the evening sets in the western horizon by His command? If you are the ruler over the expanse of the world, reverse this matter tomorrow, so that the sun rises from the west and sets in the east."

Nimrod was so bewildered that he lost the ability to speak before him (Al-Baqarah, verse 258).

Undoubtedly, Ibrahim knew that Nimrod was using sophistry in his claim of power over life and death, but his skill in argumentation did not allow this point—which had room for the sophist enemy to cling to—to be pursued. Therefore, he immediately abandoned it and seized upon something in which the enemy had no power to struggle!

The Migration of Ibrahim

Eventually, the government apparatus of the tyrant Nimrod felt that this youth was gradually becoming a center of danger for his autocratic rule, and his eloquent tongue, capable mind, and expressive logic might lead to the awakening and awareness of the oppressed masses, who might eventually break his chains of colonialism and revolt against him. He decided, by fanning the ignorant prejudices of the idolaters, to finish Ibrahim's work and, with special ceremonies that will be mentioned in Surah Al-Anbiya, burn him before the eyes of everyone in a sea of fire kindled by the people's ignorance and the ruling regime's crime, thereby putting his mind at ease forever.

But when the fire turned cold by Allah's command and Ibrahim emerged safely from that scene, such a tremor struck Nimrod's apparatus that he completely lost his morale. For Ibrahim was no longer just an adventurous and divisive youth—the label Nimrod's apparatus had given him—but had emerged as a divine leader and a brave hero who could charge alone and empty-handed against a multitude of powerful tyrants! For this reason, Nimrod and his courtiers, who sucked the blood of the poor like leeches, decided to stand against Ibrahim with all their might to continue their rule and not rest until they destroyed him.

On the other hand, Ibrahim had taken his share from that group; that is, the ready hearts had believed in him. He saw it better to leave the land of Babylon with the group of believers and supporters and set out toward the Levant, Palestine, and Egypt—the land of the Pharaohs—to spread the call of Truth. He was able to preach the reality of Monotheism in those regions and call many believers to the worship of the One God.

The Final Stage of Ibrahim's Mission

Ibrahim spent a lifetime struggling against idol worship in all its forms, especially "man-worship." He was able to illuminate the hearts of those ready with the light of Monotheism, breathe new life into human beings, and liberate many groups from the chains of autocrats.

Now he must step into the final stage of servitude and worship of God and place everything he has on the tray of sincerity and offer it to His presence, so that through the great divine trials, he may enter the stage of Imamate and leadership of humanity with a great spiritual leap.

Concurrent with this, he must raise the foundations of the House of Monotheism, the Kaaba, and turn it into a unique center of God-worship, inviting all ready believers to a great congress beside this great House of Monotheism.

The story of the jealousy of "Sarah," his first wife, toward "Hagar," the handmaid he had taken as a wife and from whom he had a son named "Ishmael," led to him taking this mother and nursing infant, by Allah's command, from the land of "Palestine" to the dry and scorched desert of "Mecca" amidst those rough and harsh mountains.

And he left them in that land where not even a drop of water could be found, by Allah's command and as a great trial, and returned.

The emergence of the spring of "Zamzam," the arrival of the tribe of "Jurhum" in that land, and their seeking permission to live in that region from Hagar—each of which is a long and detailed story—led to the habitation of this land.

Ibrahim had asked Allah to make that spot a prosperous and blessed city and to turn the hearts of the people toward his children, who were increasing in that region [^1]. Interestingly, some historians have narrated that when Ibrahim left Hagar and the nursing Ishmael in Mecca and wanted to return, Hagar called out to him: "O Ibrahim! Who commanded you to leave us in a land where there is neither vegetation, nor a nursing animal, nor even a drop of water, and that without provisions or a companion?!" Ibrahim replied in one short sentence: "My Lord has so commanded me."

When Hagar heard this sentence, she said: "Now that it is so, Allah will never leave us to ourselves!" [^1]

Ibrahim came from Palestine to Mecca "repeatedly" with the intention of visiting Ishmael. It was during one of these trips that he performed the Hajj rituals and, by Allah's command, took his son Ishmael—who had become a brave, exceptionally pure, and faithful youth—to the place of sacrifice. He was prepared to sacrifice this best fruit of the branch of his life with his own hand in the path of Allah.

When he passed this most important trial in the most excellent manner and showed his readiness to the final stage, Allah accepted his sacrifice, preserved Ishmael for him, and sent a ram as a sacrifice for him [^2].

Finally, Ibrahim, by fulfilling the right of all these examinations and emerging from the furnace of all these trials, ascended to the highest station a human can possibly reach. As the Quran says: "And [recall] when Ibrahim was tried by his Lord with commands and he fulfilled them. [Allah] said, 'Indeed, I will make you a leader (Imam) for the people.' Ibrahim, overjoyed by this news, requested: 'And of my descendants?' His prayer was answered, but with the condition that: 'My covenant does not include the wrongdoers'" [^1].

The Lofty Station of Ibrahim in the Quran

A study of the Quranic verses shows that Allah has placed Ibrahim in an exceptionally lofty station—a station He did not grant to any of the preceding prophets.

The greatness of this divine Prophet's station can be clearly understood from the following expressions:

  1. Allah has referred to Ibrahim as an "Ummah" (a nation), praising his personality as being equivalent to an entire nation (An-Nahl, 120).
  1. He granted him the station of Khalil-Allah (Friend of Allah): (وَ اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ إِبْراهِيمَ خَلِيلًا) "And Allah took Ibrahim as a friend" (An-Nisa, 125). Interestingly, in some narrations interpreting this verse, we read: "This station was because Ibrahim never asked anything of anyone and never turned away a petitioner!" [^2]
  1. He was among the righteous [^3], the virtuous [^4], the devout [^5], the truthful [^6], the forbearing [^7], and those who fulfilled their covenant [^1].
  1. Ibrahim was exceptionally hospitable [^2], such that in some narrations it is mentioned he was nicknamed Abu al-Adiyaf (the father of guests or the master of guests) [^3].
  1. He possessed unparalleled trust (tawakkul), to the extent that in no matter or event did he have any regard for anyone but Allah. Whatever he wanted, he sought from Him, and he knocked on no door but His [^4]. The story of the angels' offer to save him when the stubborn people wanted to throw him into a sea of fire, and Ibrahim's refusal of this offer, is recorded in history. He would say: "I am needy from head to toe, but not to the creature, only to the Creator!" [^5]
  1. He possessed unparalleled courage and stood alone against the roaring flood of the idolaters' prejudices, harboring not the slightest fear or terror. He mocked their idols, turned their temple into a heap of dust, and spoke with matchless bravery before Nimrod and his henchmen—each of which is mentioned in verses of the Holy Quran.
  1. Ibrahim possessed an exceptionally powerful logic, expressed in short and firm phrases.

...and he would provide crushing, reasoned responses to the misguided, and with that same eloquent logic, he would rub the noses of the stubborn in the dust.

He never lost his temper due to the intensity of their violence; rather, he faced them with a composure that reflected his great soul. Through his speech and conduct, he would hand them the very evidence of their own condemnation. This is clearly seen in the story of Ibrahim’s (عليه السلام) debate with Nimrod, with his uncle Azar, and with the judges of the court of Babylon when they sought to convict him for the "crime" of worshipping God and breaking the idols.

Pay close attention to the following verses from Surah Al-Anbiya:

When the judges asked him: "Is it you who has done this to our gods, O Ibrahim?" (قَالُوا أَأَنْتَ فَعَلْتَ هَذَا بِآلِهَتِنَا يَا إِبْرَاهِيمُ).

In response, in order to place them in a severe deadlock—a deadlock from which they would have no way of escape—he said: "Perhaps it was this, the largest of them, that did it? So ask them, if they can speak" (قَالَ بَلْ فَعَلَهُ كَبِيرُهُمْ هَذَا فَاسْأَلُوهُمْ إِنْ كَانُوا يَنْطِقُونَ).

With this single sentence, he placed his enemies in a severe impasse. If they were to say the idols are mute and tight-lipped, unable to speak, then what a disgrace to have such a dumb and incompetent god! And if they accepted that they are capable of speech, then they must ask and receive an answer! It was here that their dormant consciences were slightly awakened, and they returned to their true selves. They heard a cry from within saying, "Indeed, you are the wrongdoers, the selfish, and the oppressors"—you show mercy neither to yourselves nor to the society to which you belong (فَرَجَعُوا إِلَى أَنْفُسِهِمْ فَقَالُوا إِنَّكُمْ أَنْتُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ).

However, in any case, they felt they had to say something; thus, with utter humiliation, they said: "You already know that these [idols] do not speak" (ثُمَّ نُكِسُوا عَلَى رُؤُوسِهِمْ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَا هَؤُلَاءِ يَنْطِقُونَ).

It was at this moment that Ibrahim’s (عليه السلام) crushing words descended upon them like a thunderbolt. He cried out: "Fie upon you and upon that which you worship instead of Allah! Do you not then use your intellect?" (أُفٍّ لَكُمْ وَلِمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ).

Ultimately, since they found in themselves no power to resist Ibrahim’s (عليه السلام) mighty logic—as is the way of all tyrannical bullies—they resorted to force and said: "He must be burned."

To accomplish this, they sought help from the ignorant prejudices of the idolaters and shouted: "Hurry to the aid of your gods, if you have any power or ability!" (قَالُوا حَرِّقُوهُ وَانْصُرُوا آلِهَتَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فَاعِلِينَ) [1]. This was one example of Ibrahim’s (عليه السلام) eloquent, reasoned, decisive, and cutting logic.

8- It is noteworthy that the Quran considers it one of the honors of Muslims that they follow the religion of Ibrahim [2], and it was he who named you "Muslims" [3]. Even to encourage Muslims to perform certain important commands, He reminds them that they must emulate Ibrahim (عليه السلام) and his companions [4].

9- The Hajj pilgrimage, with all its grandeur and glory, was established by Ibrahim (عليه السلام) by the command of Allah. For this reason, the name and memory of Ibrahim (عليه السلام) are intertwined with all the rituals of Hajj [1]. In every station and every program of these great rites, man is reminded of this Divine Prophet and feels the radiance of his greatness in his heart. Fundamentally, performing the Hajj rituals without the memory of Ibrahim (عليه السلام) is meaningless!

10- Ibrahim’s (عليه السلام) personality was of such a stature that every group attempted to claim him as their own. The Jews and the Christians each emphasized their connection to Ibrahim (عليه السلام), to which the Quran responded by stating the reality that he was solely a true Muslim and monotheist—meaning one who was submitted to Allah’s command in everything, thought of nothing but Him, and walked in no path but His [2].
